prompt: Some people think that all university students should study whatever they like. Others believe they should only be allowed to study subjects that are useful in the future, such as science and technology. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
band6:
Nowadays many people argue about what students should study at university. Some people think students can study what they like, but other people think they should study useful subjects like science. In my opinion both sides have good points.

On the one hand, if students study what they like, they will be happy and work hard. For example, a student who loves art will enjoy the classes and get good marks. This is good for them.

On the other hand, science and technology subjects are very useful for the country. They can help to make new things and find good jobs. So the government wants more students in these subjects.

In conclusion, I think students should study what they like but also think about jobs. Both views are important and students must choose carefully.
band8:
The question of how much freedom students should have in choosing their degrees is a contested one. While some maintain that young people should pursue whatever discipline appeals to them, others insist that funding should favour fields with clear economic value. In my view, personal interest should take precedence, though not without regard for employability.

Those who defend free choice argue, persuasively, that motivation is the strongest predictor of academic success. A student who is genuinely passionate about literature or history is likely to engage more deeply and graduate with stronger analytical skills than one coerced into an unloved subject.

Nevertheless, advocates of a utilitarian approach have a point: economies depend on graduates in engineering, medicine and computing, and steering students toward these areas can address skills shortages and reduce graduate unemployment.

On balance, I believe the two priorities can be reconciled. Universities should preserve freedom of choice while offering robust careers guidance, so that students pursue their interests with a realistic understanding of the labour market.
gap: States a clear position in the introduction rather than 'both sides have good points'.
Uses precise lexis (contested, predictor, utilitarian, reconciled) instead of 'good points / very useful'.
Each body paragraph is developed with a reason AND a consequence, not one short claim.
Cohesion is varied (Those who defend, Nevertheless, On balance) rather than repeated 'On the one/other hand'.
The conclusion proposes a synthesis (freedom + careers guidance) instead of just restating.
phrases: the question of … is a contested one

prompt: Some people believe that individuals can do nothing to improve the environment and that only governments and large companies can make a difference. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
These days the environment is a big problem. Some people say that only governments and big companies can help, and normal people cannot do anything. I do not agree with this idea.

Firstly, it is true that companies make a lot of pollution. Factories produce smoke and governments make laws. So they have a big responsibility for the environment.

However, normal people can also do many things. They can recycle, use less plastic and save electricity. If everyone does this, it will make a big difference. For example, in my city many people use bicycles now and the air is cleaner.

In conclusion, I disagree that individuals can do nothing. Both governments and people must work together to protect the environment.
band8:
It is sometimes claimed that environmental damage can only be tackled by states and corporations, and that ordinary citizens are powerless to help. I strongly disagree with this view: while institutions undeniably bear the greatest responsibility, individual choices, multiplied across millions of households, are far from trivial.

Admittedly, governments and large firms control the levers that matter most. Legislation on emissions, investment in renewable energy and the design of public transport lie entirely beyond the reach of any single person, and without such structural reform individual effort alone cannot avert climate change.

That said, to conclude that individuals are helpless is mistaken. Consumer behaviour shapes corporate strategy: when households reject single-use plastics or favour energy-efficient appliances, manufacturers respond. Moreover, citizens vote, protest and influence others, thereby pressuring the very institutions said to hold all the power.

In conclusion, although the principal burden rests with governments and industry, individuals are neither powerless nor blameless. Meaningful progress depends on the two acting in concert.
gap: Takes a clear stance ('I strongly disagree') and sustains it throughout.
Concedes the opposing view fairly ('Admittedly…') before rebutting it — a band-8 discussion move.
Replaces vague 'big problem / big difference' with precise phrases (levers that matter, structural reform, in concert).
Develops a causal chain (consumer behaviour → corporate strategy) rather than a list.
Grammar range: conditionals, passives and relative clauses are controlled and varied.

prompt: Some people say that the increasing use of computers and smartphones to communicate has had a negative effect on young people's reading and writing skills. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Today young people use computers and smartphones all the time. Some people think this is bad for their reading and writing. I partly agree with this.

On one side, young people send short messages with bad spelling and short forms like 'u' and 'gr8'. So they forget how to write correctly and they do not read books anymore. This is a problem for their studies.

On the other side, technology can also help. Young people read a lot on the internet and they write messages every day. So they practise reading and writing in a new way.

In conclusion, I think technology has some bad effects but also good effects on reading and writing skills.
band8:
There is a widespread concern that constant reliance on digital devices is eroding the literacy of the young. While I accept that informal messaging encourages careless habits, I do not believe the overall effect is as damaging as critics suggest.

It is certainly true that text-based communication rewards brevity and abbreviation. Teenagers who routinely write in fragments, omit punctuation and lean on autocorrect may struggle to produce the sustained, formal prose that academic work demands. To this extent, the anxiety is justified.

However, this pessimistic view overlooks how much reading and writing technology actually generates. Young people consume vast quantities of text online and compose messages, posts and comments daily. Arguably, they write far more than previous generations did, even if the register differs. What matters is not the medium but whether schools teach learners to switch deliberately between casual and formal styles.

In conclusion, although digital communication can foster sloppy habits, it need not weaken literacy. With proper guidance, the same technology can enrich it.
gap: Nuanced thesis ('not as damaging as critics suggest') rather than 'I partly agree'.
Concrete, specific detail (fragments, omit punctuation, autocorrect) replaces 'bad spelling'.
Introduces the idea of register-switching — a genuine insight that lifts task response.
Discourse markers (To this extent, Arguably, What matters is) signal a controlled argument.
Avoids listy structure; each paragraph builds a position.
phrases: there is a widespread concern that

prompt: Some people believe that it is best to work for the same company for one's entire career, while others think it is better to change jobs frequently. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Some people work in one company for all their life, but other people change their jobs many times. Both ways have good and bad things. I will discuss both and give my opinion.

People who stay in one company get many benefits. They know the company well and they can get promotion. Also the company trusts them. This gives them security.

People who change jobs also have advantages. They learn new skills and meet new people. They can also get more money in a new job. So changing jobs is exciting.

In my opinion, changing jobs is better because you learn more and earn more money. But staying is also good for security.
band8:
Career paths have changed dramatically, and people now disagree about whether loyalty to a single employer or frequent moves serves a worker best. Both strategies carry clear merits, but I lean toward the view that periodic change is more rewarding in a modern economy.

Remaining with one organisation has obvious appeal. Long-serving employees accumulate deep institutional knowledge, enjoy job security and are often first in line for promotion. Such stability can also translate into generous pensions and a strong professional reputation built over decades.

Those who change roles regularly, however, reap different rewards. Each move tends to bring a salary increase, exposure to fresh challenges and a broader skill set that makes a candidate more resilient if their industry contracts. In fast-moving fields such as technology, mobility is arguably essential to staying current.

Weighing these considerations, I believe frequent change generally outweighs lifelong loyalty, since it accelerates learning and earning. That said, the ideal balance depends on the individual's priorities and the stability of their sector.
gap: Clear opinion stated up front and qualified at the end (depends on sector/priorities).
Precise collocations (institutional knowledge, salary increase, skill set, staying current) replace 'good things / more money'.
Each view is developed with three distinct supports, not one.
Sophisticated framing verbs: accumulate, reap, translate into, outweigh.
Conditional nuance about industries contracting shows critical thinking.

prompt: Prevention is better than cure. Out of a country's health budget, a large proportion should be spent on health education and preventative measures rather than treatment. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
It is often said that prevention is better than cure. Some people think the government should spend more money on health education and not on treatment. I agree with this idea.

First, if people know about healthy living, they will not get sick. For example, if they know smoking is bad, they will stop smoking. Then they will be healthy and the country saves money.

Second, treatment is very expensive. Hospitals and medicines cost a lot of money. If people do not get sick, the government does not spend this money.

In conclusion, I agree that spending on prevention is better because it keeps people healthy and saves money for the country.
band8:
The proverb 'prevention is better than cure' is frequently invoked in debates about healthcare funding, with many arguing that education and early intervention deserve the lion's share of the budget. I broadly agree, though treatment can never be neglected entirely.

The case for prioritising prevention is compelling. Many of the most costly illnesses — heart disease, type-2 diabetes, certain cancers — are strongly linked to lifestyle, and public-health campaigns that discourage smoking, promote exercise and improve diet can reduce their incidence dramatically. Money spent persuading citizens to live healthily therefore yields enormous long-term savings, easing the burden on overstretched hospitals.

Nevertheless, it would be reckless to divert funds away from treatment altogether. Accidents, genetic conditions and infectious diseases will always require well-resourced hospitals, and patients cannot be told that prevention failed them.

In conclusion, while I firmly support channelling a substantial share of spending into prevention, because it is both humane and economical, this should complement rather than replace investment in treatment.
gap: Qualified agreement ('broadly agree, though…') is more sophisticated than flat 'I agree'.
Specific evidence (heart disease, type-2 diabetes, smoking campaigns) replaces 'if people are healthy'.
Uses the economic concept of long-term savings and 'overstretched hospitals'.
Counterbalance paragraph prevents an over-simple answer — key for Task Response.
Idiomatic precision: the lion's share, yields savings, ease the burden.

prompt: Some people think that the best way to reduce crime is to give longer prison sentences. Others, however, believe there are better alternative ways. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Crime is a serious problem in many countries. Some people think long prison sentences can stop crime, but other people think there are better ways. I think both can work together.

People who support long sentences say that criminals will be afraid. If the punishment is hard, people will not do crime. Also, criminals stay in prison so they cannot do crime again.

Other people say that prison does not solve the real problem. They think education and jobs are better. If poor people have jobs, they will not steal. Also, prisons are very expensive.

In my opinion, long sentences are necessary for serious crimes, but for small crimes, education and jobs are better solutions.
band8:
How best to curb crime is a perennial debate. One camp favours tougher, longer custodial sentences, while another insists that rehabilitation and social reform are more effective. In my view, the second approach should predominate, though harsh penalties retain a place for the gravest offences.

Proponents of lengthy sentences argue that they deter potential offenders and incapacitate dangerous ones, keeping the public safe. There is some truth in this, particularly for violent crime, where removing a perpetrator from society is plainly justified.

However, the evidence that long sentences reduce overall crime is weak. Reoffending rates remain stubbornly high among released prisoners, suggesting that incarceration alone changes little. Tackling the roots of crime — poverty, addiction, lack of education and employment — is likely to prove far more durable, since it prevents offending before it begins rather than merely punishing it afterwards.

On balance, therefore, I believe prevention and rehabilitation should lead policy, with imprisonment reserved for those who genuinely endanger others.
gap: A ranked opinion ('the second should predominate, though…') rather than 'both can work together'.
Field-specific lexis: custodial sentences, deter, incapacitate, reoffending rates, rehabilitation.
Engages with evidence (reoffending rates remain high) instead of assertion.
Addresses root causes analytically, naming four concrete drivers of crime.
Cohesion through contrast and concession (There is some truth in this… However…).
phrases: how best to curb crime is a perennial debate

prompt: Some people think that globalisation has a positive effect on culture, while others believe it threatens local traditions. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
band6:
Globalisation is happening in all countries now. Some people think it is good for culture, but other people think it is bad for local traditions. I will discuss both views.

Some people say globalisation is good because people can learn about other cultures. They can eat different food and watch films from other countries. This makes life more interesting and people understand each other.

Other people say globalisation is bad because local traditions disappear. Big companies sell the same products everywhere and young people forget their own culture. For example, they prefer fast food instead of traditional food.

In my opinion, globalisation has both effects, but I think the good effects are more important because people learn from each other.
band8:
As national borders grow more porous, opinion is divided over whether globalisation enriches culture or erodes cherished traditions. Having weighed both positions, I believe its cultural impact is, on balance, positive, provided communities make an effort to safeguard their heritage.

Those who welcome globalisation point to an unprecedented exchange of ideas, cuisines and art forms. People today can savour foreign films, music and food without leaving home, and this exposure tends to foster tolerance and mutual understanding between societies that were once isolated.

Critics counter that this exchange is far from equal. Powerful global brands and dominant languages can overwhelm local customs, so that traditional crafts, dialects and festivals slowly fade as younger generations gravitate toward a homogenised global culture.

While I take this concern seriously, I would argue that openness and preservation are not mutually exclusive. With deliberate support for local languages and arts, societies can absorb the benefits of global culture while keeping their distinctiveness intact.
gap: Sophisticated thesis with a condition ('provided communities safeguard their heritage').
Lexical precision: porous borders, unprecedented exchange, homogenised, gravitate toward.
The counter-view about unequal exchange shows critical depth.
Concludes that the two forces are 'not mutually exclusive' — a nuanced resolution.
Wider range of verbs: savour, foster, overwhelm, absorb.

prompt: Some people believe the news media has become more influential in people's lives today and that this is a negative development. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
The news media is very powerful today. Many people think this is a bad thing. I agree that the media has too much power and this is negative.

Firstly, the media can control what people think. They choose which news to show and they can make people angry or afraid. This is dangerous for society.

Secondly, a lot of news today is not true. Some media want money so they make fake news to get more readers. People believe this news and they make wrong decisions.

In conclusion, I agree that the media has become too influential and this has a negative effect because it controls opinions and spreads fake news.
band8:
Few would deny that the news media now occupies a central place in daily life. Whether this growing influence is harmful, however, is debatable. While I acknowledge the genuine dangers, I would argue the picture is more balanced than the wholly negative verdict suggests.

There are legitimate grounds for concern. Outlets compete fiercely for attention, and this commercial pressure can encourage sensationalism, selective reporting and even outright misinformation. When audiences absorb such content uncritically, public opinion can be distorted and social divisions deepened.

Yet to condemn the media outright is to ignore its indispensable functions. A free press exposes corruption, holds the powerful to account and equips citizens with the information democracy requires. The very reach that makes the media dangerous also makes it a formidable check on abuses of power.

In conclusion, although the modern media is undeniably influential and sometimes irresponsible, I do not regard its rise as simply negative. The remedy lies in stronger standards and a more discerning audience, not in diminishing its role.
gap: Refuses the loaded premise and argues a balanced position — strong Task Response.
Precise vocabulary: sensationalism, hold the powerful to account, a discerning audience.
Recognises both the watchdog role and the risks rather than one side.
Advanced structures: 'The very reach that makes… also makes…'.
Conclusion offers a remedy instead of repeating the prompt.

prompt: In some countries, an increasing number of people are choosing to live alone. What are the reasons for this, and is it a positive or negative development?
band6:
In many countries today, more people live alone. There are some reasons for this and I think it is both positive and negative.

One reason is that people have more money now. They can buy or rent their own house and they do not need to live with family. Another reason is that people marry later and some people do not marry.

This development has good and bad sides. The good side is that people are independent and free. They can do what they want. The bad side is that people can feel lonely and have no help when they are sick.

In conclusion, living alone has both good and bad effects on people and society.
band8:
In a growing number of societies, solo living has become increasingly common. This trend stems from several intertwined causes, and although it carries certain risks, I regard it as a largely positive sign of social progress.

Several factors explain the shift. Rising prosperity allows more individuals to afford independent accommodation, while changing attitudes mean that marriage is delayed or forgone altogether. Greater career mobility also encourages people to relocate alone, and an ageing population leaves many living by themselves in later life.

The consequences are mixed but, I would argue, predominantly favourable. Living independently fosters self-reliance, privacy and the freedom to organise one's life on one's own terms — hallmarks of a society that respects individual choice. The principal drawback is the risk of isolation, particularly among the elderly, which can harm both mental and physical health.

In conclusion, the rise of solo living chiefly reflects affluence and personal freedom. Provided communities support those at risk of loneliness, I consider it a positive reflection of how modern life has evolved.
gap: Answers BOTH parts explicitly (reasons + evaluation) with a clear verdict.
Causal vocabulary: stems from, intertwined causes, career mobility, ageing population.
Evaluative nuance ('predominantly favourable', 'principal drawback').
Cohesive listing of causes without a mechanical 'firstly/secondly'.
Lexis of independence: self-reliance, on one's own terms, hallmarks.
phrases: this trend stems from several causes

prompt: International tourism has brought enormous benefits to many places. At the same time, there is concern about its impact on local inhabitants and the environment. Do the advantages of international tourism outweigh its disadvantages?
band6:
International tourism is very popular now. It has many advantages but also some disadvantages. I think the advantages are more than the disadvantages.

The main advantage is money. Tourists spend money in hotels, restaurants and shops. This creates jobs for local people and helps the economy. Also, tourism helps people from different countries to understand each other.

However, there are some disadvantages. Many tourists can damage the environment and make pollution. Also, prices become high and local people cannot buy things. Sometimes local culture is lost because of tourists.

In conclusion, tourism has some bad effects but the good effects like money and jobs are more important, so the advantages outweigh the disadvantages.
band8:
International travel has expanded rapidly, bringing prosperity to countless destinations while raising legitimate worries about residents and ecosystems. On balance, I believe its benefits outweigh its drawbacks, provided tourism is carefully managed.

The advantages are considerable. Visitor spending injects vital revenue into local economies, sustaining jobs in hospitality, transport and retail that might otherwise not exist. Tourism can also fund the conservation of heritage sites and foster cross-cultural understanding, as travellers and hosts encounter unfamiliar ways of life.

The disadvantages, however, are real. Mass tourism can overwhelm fragile environments, generating pollution and straining water and energy supplies. It may also inflate local prices, pricing residents out of their own neighbourhoods, and reduce living cultures to commercial spectacle.

Yet most of these harms stem from poor management rather than tourism itself. With visitor caps, environmental safeguards and revenue reinvested in communities, the worst effects can be mitigated. For this reason I maintain that, handled responsibly, the gains of international tourism decisively outweigh its costs.
gap: Answers the exact question ('outweigh?') with a conditional verdict.
Economic lexis: inject revenue, sustain jobs, price residents out.
Distinguishes harms caused by tourism from harms caused by poor management — key insight.
Offers concrete mitigations (visitor caps, safeguards) rather than vague claims.
Stronger collocations: fragile environments, commercial spectacle, decisively outweigh.

prompt: In some countries, young people are encouraged to work or travel for a year between finishing high school and starting university studies. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages for young people who decide to do this.
band6:
In some countries, students take one year to work or travel before university. This 'gap year' has advantages and disadvantages.

There are some advantages. Students can earn money for their studies and they get work experience. If they travel, they learn about other cultures and become more independent. This is good for their future.

But there are also disadvantages. Some students forget how to study after one year and it is difficult to start again. Also, travelling costs a lot of money and some families cannot pay for it. Some students just waste their time.

In conclusion, a gap year has both advantages and disadvantages, and it depends on the student.
band8:
In a number of countries it has become common for school-leavers to spend a year working or travelling before enrolling at university. This so-called gap year offers clear benefits, though it is not without pitfalls.

The advantages can be substantial. A year in employment allows young people to save toward tuition while gaining practical experience and workplace maturity that no classroom provides. Those who travel, meanwhile, broaden their horizons, develop independence and often return with a clearer sense of what they wish to study, having tested their assumptions against the wider world.

There are, however, genuine drawbacks. Stepping away from formal study can disrupt academic momentum, making the return to disciplined learning harder. Extensive travel can also be prohibitively expensive, placing it beyond the reach of poorer families, and without a definite purpose a gap year risks degenerating into idle drift.

In conclusion, a well-planned interlude can enrich a young person enormously, but its value hinges entirely on how purposefully the time is used.
gap: Names the maturity/clarity benefit precisely, not just 'good for the future'.
Advanced lexis: school-leavers, workplace maturity, broaden their horizons, idle drift.
Acknowledges inequality (beyond the reach of poorer families).
Conditional conclusion ('hinges on how purposefully the time is used') instead of 'it depends'.
Participial clauses ('having tested their assumptions') add grammatical range.
phrases: it has become common for school-leavers to

prompt: Many people now work from home rather than in an office. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this development for employees?
band6:
Nowadays many people work from home and not in an office. This has advantages and disadvantages for workers.

The advantages are clear. Workers do not travel to the office, so they save time and money. They can also spend more time with their family and work in a comfortable place. This makes them happy.

The disadvantages are also important. At home there are many distractions like television and family. So it is difficult to concentrate. Also, workers feel lonely because they do not meet their colleagues. Sometimes they work too much and cannot rest.

In conclusion, working from home has good and bad sides for employees.
band8:
Remote working, once a rarity, has become a fixture of professional life, and the shift carries significant implications for employees themselves.

The benefits are easy to appreciate. Eliminating the daily commute returns hours and money to workers while sparing them its stress, and many find that a flexible schedule allows a healthier balance between professional and family commitments. Freed from the interruptions of a busy office, some employees also report sharper focus and higher productivity.

The drawbacks, however, deserve equal weight. The home is full of competing demands, and without clear boundaries the working day can blur into domestic life, leaving employees perpetually 'on call'. Isolation is a further hazard: deprived of face-to-face contact with colleagues, remote workers may feel disconnected and miss the mentoring and spontaneous collaboration that an office naturally provides.

In conclusion, home working offers employees welcome flexibility and autonomy, yet it can erode boundaries and breed isolation. Whether it helps or harms ultimately depends on the individual's discipline and the support their employer provides.
gap: Specific benefit/harm pairs (focus vs blurred boundaries; flexibility vs isolation).
Idiomatic precision: a fixture of professional life, perpetually 'on call', spontaneous collaboration.
Acknowledges loss of mentoring — a non-obvious, mature point.
Conditional close tied to discipline and employer support.
Controlled participials and contrast ('Freed from…', 'deprived of…').
phrases: once a rarity, has become a fixture

prompt: Many goods are now produced to last only a short time before they need to be replaced. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this trend?
band6:
Today many products do not last a long time. After a short time they break and people buy new ones. This trend has advantages and disadvantages.

One advantage is for the economy. When people buy new products, companies sell more and make money. This creates jobs for workers. Also, new products have new technology, so they are better.

But there are disadvantages too. This trend is bad for the environment because there is a lot of waste. People throw away old products and this makes pollution. Also, people spend a lot of money to buy new things again and again.

In conclusion, this trend is good for the economy but bad for the environment and people's money.
band8:
It is increasingly the case that consumer goods are designed with short lifespans, obliging buyers to replace them frequently. This phenomenon, often called planned obsolescence, brings certain economic gains but exacts a heavy environmental and personal toll.

On the positive side, rapid replacement cycles stimulate the economy. Constant demand keeps factories busy, sustains employment and accelerates innovation, since manufacturers must continually refine their products to entice repeat purchases. Consumers, in turn, gain regular access to the latest features and improved designs.

The disadvantages, however, are difficult to ignore. Discarding products after only a few years generates mountains of waste, much of it toxic and non-recyclable, intensifying the strain on an already burdened planet. Households also bear the cost, trapped in a cycle of repeated spending that benefits corporations far more than the people who fund it.

In conclusion, while disposable manufacturing fuels growth and innovation, its environmental damage and financial burden are considerable. A shift toward durable, repairable goods would serve society far better in the long run.
gap: Names the concept (planned obsolescence) — precise framing.
Economic and environmental lexis: replacement cycles, entice repeat purchases, an already burdened planet.
Goes beyond 'good for economy/bad for environment' to who benefits vs who pays.
Proposes a forward-looking solution (durable, repairable goods).
Stronger verbs: oblige, exact a toll, stimulate, entice, intensify.
phrases: it is increasingly the case that

prompt: More and more people are migrating to cities in search of a better life, but city life can be extremely difficult. Explain some of the difficulties of living in a city. How can governments make urban life better for everyone?
band6:
Many people move to cities to find a better life. But living in a city is not easy and there are many problems. Governments must help to solve them.

There are some difficulties in cities. First, there is a lot of traffic and pollution. Second, houses are very expensive and some people cannot find a place to live. Also, there is crime and people feel unsafe.

Governments can do many things to make city life better. They can build more public transport like buses and trains to reduce traffic. They can build cheap houses for poor people. Also, they can hire more police to stop crime.

In conclusion, city life has many difficulties, but if governments take action, they can make cities better for everyone.
band8:
The lure of better prospects continues to draw vast numbers of people into cities, yet urban living poses formidable challenges. Below I outline the most pressing difficulties and suggest how governments might alleviate them.

City dwellers face several acute problems. Foremost is the cost of housing, which in many metropolises has spiralled beyond the reach of ordinary workers, forcing them into cramped or distant accommodation. Congestion and the pollution it generates are equally damaging, eroding both health and quality of life, while overstretched services and rising crime can leave residents feeling unsafe and unsupported.

Governments, however, are far from powerless. Substantial investment in efficient public transport would ease congestion and curb emissions, and the construction of subsidised housing would relieve the affordability crisis. Pairing visible policing with social programmes that tackle the roots of crime, such as unemployment, would address insecurity more durably than enforcement alone.

In conclusion, although cities can be daunting places to live, well-targeted public policy, from transport to housing to community safety, can render urban life far more liveable for all.
gap: Signposts the two-part task ('outline the difficulties… suggest how governments…').
Urban lexis: metropolises, congestion, subsidised housing, affordability crisis.
Solutions are matched directly to the problems raised, not generic.
Adds the idea of tackling crime's roots, not just policing.
Sophisticated framing: spiralled beyond reach, render urban life liveable.
phrases: render urban life far more liveable

prompt: Many countries are facing a shortage of fresh water. What are the causes of this problem, and what measures could be taken to solve it?
band6:
Many countries do not have enough fresh water today. This is a big problem with many causes, but there are also some solutions.

There are different causes. One cause is that the population is growing, so people need more water. Another cause is climate change because there is less rain. Also, people waste a lot of water and farmers use too much water for crops.

There are also some solutions. Governments can teach people to save water and not waste it. They can also build better systems to clean and reuse water. Farmers can use new technology to use less water.

In conclusion, water shortage is caused by many things, but with education and technology, we can solve this problem.
band8:
Freshwater scarcity has emerged as one of the most serious challenges confronting many nations. The crisis arises from several converging causes, but a combination of practical measures could substantially relieve it.

The causes are interlinked. Population growth and rapid urbanisation have sharply increased demand, while climate change has disrupted rainfall and accelerated the depletion of rivers and aquifers. Compounding this, agriculture consumes the lion's share of available water, often through wasteful irrigation, and lax regulation allows widespread pollution and leakage to squander what remains.

Fortunately, the problem is not intractable. Governments could invest in modern infrastructure to reduce leakage and treat wastewater for reuse, and incentivise efficient drip irrigation in farming. Public-awareness campaigns and realistic pricing would further discourage casual waste, while protecting watersheds from contamination would safeguard existing supplies.

In conclusion, freshwater shortages stem chiefly from rising demand, climate disruption and inefficient use. Through investment in infrastructure, smarter agriculture and changes in behaviour, however, societies can manage this precious resource far more sustainably.
gap: Names causes precisely (aquifers, irrigation, leakage) instead of 'less rain'.
Solutions map onto causes (drip irrigation answers wasteful farming).
Technical lexis: depletion, watersheds, wastewater reuse, drip irrigation.
'not intractable' and 'converging causes' raise the register.
Conclusion synthesises causes and remedies, not a flat restatement.
phrases: one of the most serious challenges confronting
the depletion of rivers and aquifers

prompt: In many countries, the rate of obesity is increasing, especially among young people. What are the causes of this, and what solutions can you suggest?
band6:
In many countries, more people are obese, especially young people. This problem has causes and solutions.

There are some causes. First, young people eat a lot of fast food because it is cheap and tasty. Second, they do not do exercise. They sit at home and play video games or watch television all day. Also, some families do not cook healthy food.

There are some solutions too. Schools can teach children about healthy food and give them sport classes. Parents can cook healthy meals at home. The government can put a tax on fast food and sugary drinks so people buy less.

In conclusion, obesity has many causes like fast food and no exercise, but education and government action can solve it.
band8:
Rising obesity, particularly among the young, has become a pressing public-health concern across much of the world. The trend has identifiable causes, and a coordinated response could reverse it.

The causes are largely behavioural and environmental. Diets have shifted toward cheap, energy-dense fast food and sugary drinks that are aggressively marketed to children, while portion sizes have ballooned. At the same time, physical activity has plummeted: screens have replaced outdoor play, and many young people lead increasingly sedentary lives. Busy households relying on processed convenience food only compound the problem.

The remedies must be equally broad. Schools can play a pivotal role by providing nutritious meals, restoring physical education and teaching children to cook and eat well. Governments, for their part, could tax sugary products, curb junk-food advertising aimed at minors and redesign towns to encourage walking and cycling. Crucially, parents must model healthy habits at home.

In conclusion, childhood obesity stems from poor diet and inactivity, but through education, regulation and family example, it is a thoroughly reversible trend.
gap: Categorises causes (behavioural/environmental) rather than listing flatly.
Precise lexis: energy-dense, sedentary, portion sizes have ballooned, convenience food.
Solutions span school, government and family — a fuller answer.
Adds urban design (walking/cycling) — a sophisticated remedy.
Confident framing: 'a thoroughly reversible trend'.
phrases: schools can play a pivotal role

prompt: Many people believe that money is the most important factor when choosing a job. Why do people choose well-paid jobs? Is this the best way to choose a career?
band6:
Many people think money is the most important thing when they choose a job. There are reasons for this, but I think it is not the best way.

People choose well-paid jobs for some reasons. First, money helps them to buy a house, a car and good things for their family. Second, with money they feel safe and they do not worry about the future. Also, in society people respect rich people.

But I think money is not the best way to choose a career. If people only think about money, they can choose a job they do not like. Then they will not be happy and they will feel stress. It is better to choose a job that you enjoy.

In conclusion, people choose well-paid jobs for money and security, but I think it is better to enjoy your job.
band8:
When selecting a career, many people place salary above all other considerations. Although the appeal of a lucrative job is understandable, I do not believe earnings should be the decisive factor.

The reasons for chasing high pay are not hard to grasp. A generous income secures life's necessities and comforts, from housing and healthcare to opportunities for one's children. It also offers a reassuring buffer against future uncertainty, and in many societies wealth confers status and a sense of achievement. For those raised in hardship especially, financial security is an entirely rational priority.

Nonetheless, judging a career by its salary alone is, in my view, short-sighted. A demanding but well-paid role that one finds tedious or stressful can corrode both health and happiness, however large the pay cheque. Lasting fulfilment tends to flow from work that is meaningful, suited to one's strengths and conducive to a balanced life.

In conclusion, while the pursuit of a good salary is perfectly reasonable, I believe interest, purpose and well-being deserve at least equal weight when one chooses a career.
gap: Both sub-questions answered distinctly with a clear evaluative stance.
Refined lexis: a lucrative job, a reassuring buffer, confers status, corrode happiness.
Concedes the rationality of valuing money (esp. for the poor) before disagreeing.
Argues for fulfilment with specific criteria (meaningful, suited to strengths).
Balanced, qualified conclusion rather than a blunt one.
phrases: place salary above all other considerations
the appeal of a lucrative job

prompt: Some people think children should begin their formal education at a very early age, while others think they should not start school until they are at least seven years old. What is your opinion?
band6:
People have different ideas about when children should start school. Some say very early and some say at seven years old. I think starting a little later is better.

Some people think early education is good because children learn fast when they are young. They can learn reading, writing and numbers early and they will be clever. Also, parents can go to work while the child is at school.

But I think children should not start too early. Small children need to play because playing helps them to learn social skills. If they start school too early, they can feel stress and they do not enjoy childhood.

In conclusion, early education has some benefits, but I think children should start school a little later so they can play and grow happily.
band8:
Opinion is divided over the ideal age to begin formal schooling, with some favouring an early start and others a later one. Having considered both positions, I lean toward delaying formal instruction until around the age of six or seven.

Proponents of early schooling make a reasonable case. Young children are remarkably receptive, absorbing language and basic numeracy with ease, and an early start can give them a head start academically. It also enables parents, particularly mothers, to return to work sooner, easing financial pressures on the family.

I am persuaded, however, that pushing formal lessons onto very young children can do more harm than good. At that age, unstructured play is itself a powerful form of learning, nurturing imagination, social skills and emotional resilience. Forcing a rigid academic routine too soon risks dampening a child's natural curiosity and even breeding anxiety, with little lasting benefit.

In conclusion, while early education has its merits, I believe children flourish best when allowed to learn through play first and meet formal schooling only once they are developmentally ready.
gap: Clear personal opinion that directly answers 'What is your opinion?'
Developmental lexis: receptive, numeracy, unstructured play, emotional resilience.
Concedes the parental-employment benefit — a balanced, mature treatment.
Argues from child development (play as learning) rather than assertion.
Phrase precision: do more harm than good, dampen curiosity, developmentally ready.
phrases: unstructured play is a powerful form of learning

prompt: In many parts of the world, people of different generations are less likely to live together than in the past. Why is this the case? Do you think it is a positive or negative trend?
band6:
In the past, grandparents, parents and children lived together, but now this is less common. There are reasons for this and I think it is both positive and negative.

There are some reasons. First, young people move to cities or other countries to find jobs, so they live far from their parents. Second, people want privacy and freedom, so they prefer to live alone. Also, modern houses are smaller now.

I think this trend is both positive and negative. The positive side is that people are independent and have privacy. The negative side is that old people can be lonely and children cannot learn from their grandparents.

In conclusion, families live separately for jobs and privacy, and this has both good and bad effects.
band8:
Across much of the world, the multigenerational household, once the norm, is in decline. This shift has several explanations, and although it brings real benefits, I regard its overall impact as mildly negative.

The reasons are chiefly economic and cultural. Globalised labour markets compel the young to move to cities or abroad in pursuit of work, leaving parents and grandparents behind. Rising individualism, meanwhile, has made privacy and autonomy more prized than ever, and the smaller dwellings typical of urban life simply cannot accommodate an extended family under one roof.

While greater independence is undeniably welcome, I am inclined to see more loss than gain. Elderly relatives who once enjoyed the company and care of their families increasingly face isolation, and children forgo the wisdom, stories and informal childcare that grandparents traditionally provide. Society as a whole loses a web of mutual support that no institution fully replaces.

In conclusion, families now live apart largely because of economic migration and a thirst for independence; on balance, I find this a regrettable, if understandable, development.
gap: Both parts answered with a clearly weighted verdict ('mildly negative').
Sociological lexis: multigenerational household, individualism, economic migration.
Identifies a societal loss (web of mutual support) beyond individual effects.
Concedes the benefit of independence before judging — nuanced.
Refined connectors: meanwhile, while… I am inclined to, on balance.
phrases: the multigenerational household is in decline
globalised labour markets compel the young
privacy and autonomy more prized than ever

prompt: Some people think that scientific research should be carried out and controlled by governments rather than private companies. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think governments should control scientific research and not private companies. I partly agree with this idea.

On one hand, government research is good for society. The government does research to help people, for example in health and education, and not only for money. Also, the government can share the results with everyone.

On the other hand, private companies are also important. They have a lot of money and they can do research faster. Many new technologies, like phones and medicines, come from private companies.

In conclusion, I think both governments and companies should do research together, because the government thinks about society and companies bring money and speed.
band8:
It is sometimes argued that scientific research ought to be conducted and overseen by the state rather than by private enterprise. While I see the appeal of public stewardship, I disagree that governments should monopolise research; the two sectors are most powerful in partnership.

There is a sound case for government involvement. Publicly funded science can pursue goals that serve the common good, such as curing disease or addressing climate change, without the distorting pressure to generate profit. The state can also ensure that findings are shared openly and that ethical limits are respected.

Nevertheless, to exclude private companies would be a serious mistake. Firms possess the capital, agility and commercial incentive to translate discoveries into usable products with remarkable speed; much of the technology and medicine we rely on originated in corporate laboratories. Stifling that dynamism would slow innovation and waste valuable expertise.

In conclusion, although government oversight is vital for safeguarding the public interest, research should not be its exclusive preserve. A balanced system that harnesses public funding and private ingenuity will achieve far more than either could alone.
gap: Reframes 'partly agree' into a precise position (oversight yes, monopoly no).
Higher lexis: public stewardship, the distorting pressure to profit, commercial incentive.
Develops the private-sector case with three distinct strengths (capital, agility, incentive).
Conclusion argues for synthesis with reasons, not a vague 'work together'.
Grammar range: concessive clauses and nominalisation used accurately.
phrases: the distorting pressure to generate profit
harness public funding and private ingenuity

prompt: Some people think that governments should spend money on public services and infrastructure rather than on the arts, such as music and painting. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think the government should spend money on public services and not on the arts like music and painting. I do not fully agree with this idea.

It is true that public services are very important. People need hospitals, schools and roads. If the government does not spend on these, people will suffer. So this should be the first priority.

However, the arts are also important for society. Music and painting make people happy and they are part of our culture. Also, the arts can bring money from tourism. So the government should not stop spending on them.

In conclusion, I agree that public services are more important, but the government should still spend some money on the arts.
band8:
It is often contended that public money is better spent on essential services and infrastructure than on the arts. While I accept that necessities must come first, I disagree that culture should therefore be starved of funding.

Clearly, no government can neglect the foundations of a decent society. Hospitals, schools, clean water and reliable transport are indispensable, and where resources are scarce these must take priority over discretionary spending. Few would dispute that a child's education matters more than a new concert hall.

Yet to dismiss the arts as a luxury is short-sighted. Music, painting and theatre enrich national life, preserve heritage and foster the creativity on which modern economies increasingly depend. Cultural institutions also draw tourists and sustain countless jobs, so the spending frequently repays itself. A society that funds only the strictly practical risks becoming impoverished in spirit even as it prospers materially.

In conclusion, although public services rightly command the larger share of the budget, I firmly believe the arts deserve continued, if modest, public support, for they nourish a dimension of life that material provision alone cannot reach.
gap: Distinguishes 'necessities first' from 'starve the arts' — a precise position.
Economic and cultural lexis: discretionary spending, repays itself, impoverished in spirit.
Argues the arts' economic value (tourism, jobs) AND intrinsic value.
Memorable contrast: impoverished in spirit even as it prospers materially.
Concession-led structure with strong cohesion.
phrases: the foundations of a decent society
dismiss the arts as a luxury

prompt: Some people think that the government should build more roads to help reduce traffic congestion, while others believe this will only encourage more cars. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Traffic congestion is a big problem in many cities. Some people think the government should build more roads, but others think this will bring more cars. I think building roads is not the best solution.

People who want more roads say that if there is more space, cars can move easily and there will be less traffic. New roads can also connect cities and help business.

But other people say that more roads bring more cars. When there are new roads, more people buy cars and use them, so after some time there is traffic again. It is better to use public transport.

In my opinion, building roads is not enough. The government should also build good public transport like buses and trains so people do not use cars.
band8:
Urban congestion provokes endless debate, with some insisting that more roads are the cure and others warning that new roads merely invite more traffic. Having weighed both arguments, I side firmly with the sceptics: expanding road capacity alone is self-defeating.

The case for road-building seems intuitive. More lanes promise to ease bottlenecks, shorten journeys and improve the flow of goods, thereby boosting economic activity. In genuinely under-served areas, new connections can indeed bring real benefits.

However, abundant evidence supports the opposing view. The phenomenon of 'induced demand' means that extra capacity quickly fills up: as driving becomes easier, more people choose to drive, and within a few years the roads are as clogged as before. Pouring money into asphalt can therefore entrench car dependency while doing little for long-term mobility.

In my opinion, the durable solution lies elsewhere. Rather than chasing congestion with ever more tarmac, governments should invest heavily in reliable public transport, cycling networks and pricing schemes that discourage unnecessary car use. Only by reducing demand, not just expanding supply, can cities move freely.
gap: Names 'induced demand' — a precise, evidence-based concept.
Higher lexis: bottlenecks, entrench car dependency, durable solution, mobility.
Concedes the road-building case fairly before rebutting with evidence.
Conclusion offers concrete alternatives (transit, cycling, pricing).
Memorable framing: 'reducing demand, not just expanding supply'.
phrases: reduce demand, not just expand supply

prompt: In the future, more people will speak the same language because of globalisation. Some people think this is a positive development, while others disagree. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
band6:
Because of globalisation, in the future maybe everyone will speak the same language, for example English. Some people think this is good, but others think it is bad. I will discuss both views.

Some people think one language is good. If everyone speaks the same language, they can communicate easily. Business, travel and study will be easier and there will be less misunderstanding between countries.

But other people think it is bad. If everyone speaks one language, small languages will disappear. Language is part of culture, so people will lose their identity and traditions. This is a big loss for the world.

In my opinion, one common language is useful, but we should also keep our own languages so we do not lose our culture.
band8:
Globalisation may, in time, lead much of humanity to converse in a single shared language. Whether this prospect is welcome divides opinion sharply, and after weighing both sides I conclude that the benefits are real but must not come at the expense of linguistic diversity.

Advocates highlight the obvious convenience of a common tongue. Frictionless communication would smooth international trade, travel, science and diplomacy, dissolving the misunderstandings that language barriers so often create and binding distant peoples more closely together.

Opponents, however, raise a profound objection. Languages are not merely tools but vessels of culture, memory and identity; as a dominant language spreads, thousands of smaller ones risk extinction, and with each one lost goes a unique way of seeing the world. Such homogenisation would impoverish humanity's collective heritage.

In my view, both truths can be honoured. Societies can embrace a global lingua franca for practical purposes while actively preserving and celebrating their native languages. The goal should be bilingual enrichment, not the silent erasure of the world's linguistic wealth.
gap: Reframes the question and states a conditional verdict, not 'both views'.
Elevated lexis: a common tongue, vessels of culture, lingua franca, homogenisation.
The 'languages as vessels of culture' argument adds genuine depth.
Resolution ('bilingual enrichment, not erasure') is precise and memorable.
Wide cohesion: Advocates… Opponents… In my view, both truths can be honoured.
phrases: may lead humanity to converse in a single language
the obvious convenience of a common tongue

prompt: Many people spend a large part of their day online, and some become addicted to the internet and social media. What problems does this cause, and what measures can be taken to address them?
band6:
Today many people spend a lot of time on the internet and social media, and some become addicted. This causes problems, but there are some solutions.

There are some problems. First, people who use the internet too much can have health problems like bad eyes and no sleep. Second, they do not meet real friends and they feel lonely. Also, students waste their time and do not study well.

There are some solutions. People should control their time and not use the phone too much. Parents should check how much time children use the internet. Also, schools can teach students about the danger of internet addiction.

In conclusion, internet addiction causes health and social problems, but with self-control and education, we can solve this problem.
band8:
A growing number of people now spend much of their waking lives online, and for some this heavy use tips into genuine addiction. The consequences are far-reaching, but a mix of personal, parental and institutional measures can help to contain them.

The problems are both physical and psychological. Compulsive scrolling disrupts sleep, strains the eyes and encourages a sedentary lifestyle, while the constant comparison fostered by social media can corrode self-esteem and fuel anxiety. Socially, ironically, those most 'connected' online often grow isolated offline, and students in particular find their concentration and academic performance eroded by perpetual distraction.

Addressing this requires effort on several fronts. Individuals can reclaim control by setting screen-time limits and switching off notifications, and parents should both monitor their children's use and model healthier habits themselves. Schools, meanwhile, can teach digital literacy that equips young people to use technology mindfully, and platforms could be obliged to design less manipulative, less addictive products.

In conclusion, internet addiction harms health, relationships and study, yet through self-discipline, parental guidance and better design, its grip can be meaningfully loosened.
gap: Categorises harms (physical/psychological/social) for clarity.
Precise lexis: compulsive scrolling, sedentary, corrode self-esteem, digital literacy.
Notes the 'connected yet isolated' irony — a mature observation.
Solutions span individual, parental, school AND platform design.
Strong framing verbs: tips into addiction, corrode, reclaim control, loosen its grip.
phrases: spend much of their waking lives online

prompt: Some people believe that children should be taught how to manage money as a school subject. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Today money is very important, so some people think children should learn how to manage money at school. I agree with this idea.

First, many young people do not know how to use money well. They spend everything quickly and they do not save. If they learn about money at school, they will be more careful in the future.

Second, this skill is useful for everyone. When children grow up, they must pay bills, save and maybe take a loan. So it is good to learn this early.

In conclusion, I agree that money management should be taught in school because it is a useful skill for life.
band8:
In an age of easy credit and constant spending, many argue that personal finance should be a formal school subject. I strongly agree, since few skills are more useful in adult life yet so rarely taught.

The central case is practical. Many young people leave school able to solve equations but unable to budget, compare loans or avoid debt. Teaching financial literacy directly would equip them to make informed decisions about saving, borrowing and spending long before costly mistakes are made.

Furthermore, such knowledge promotes wider social benefits. Citizens who understand interest, taxation and pensions are less likely to fall into ruinous debt or be exploited by predatory lenders, easing pressure on families and welfare systems alike.

Admittedly, the curriculum is already crowded, and some insist money management is best learned at home. Yet relying on parents leaves poorer children, whose families may lack such knowledge, at a lasting disadvantage. In conclusion, I firmly believe financial literacy deserves a place in every school, for it prepares the young for a reality they will all inevitably face.
gap: States a firm position and sustains it, rather than two short reasons.
Precise lexis: financial literacy, predatory lenders, ruinous debt, make informed decisions.
Adds a social-equity argument (poorer children disadvantaged) for depth.
Concedes the 'crowded curriculum / learn at home' counter before rebutting.
Varied cohesion: The central case, Furthermore, Admittedly, In conclusion.
phrases: a core part of the curriculum

prompt: Some think exams are the best way to assess students, while others prefer continuous assessment. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Schools use different ways to check students. Some people think exams are the best, but other people prefer continuous assessment. I will discuss both and give my opinion.

Some people like exams because they are fair. Every student does the same test at the same time, so it is equal. Exams also show if a student can work under pressure.

Other people prefer continuous assessment like projects and homework. They say it shows the real ability of a student over time, not just one day. Also, some students are nervous in exams and fail.

In my opinion, continuous assessment is better because it is less stressful and shows the true level of the student.
band8:
How best to assess students is a long-standing debate, with some defending traditional examinations and others favouring continuous assessment. Having weighed both, I believe a combination serves learners best, though continuous methods deserve greater weight.

Supporters of exams argue, with reason, that they are standardised and difficult to cheat: every candidate sits the same paper under the same conditions, producing a comparable measure of ability. Exams also test composure under pressure, a skill that matters in many careers.

Continuous assessment, however, captures what a single exam cannot. Coursework, presentations and projects reveal a student's progress, creativity and consistency over months rather than hours, and they spare capable but anxious learners from being undone by one stressful morning.

On balance, I find the broader picture offered by continuous assessment more reliable, but exams still guard against grade inflation and dishonesty. The fairest system, therefore, blends the two, rewarding sustained effort while preserving a rigorous, comparable benchmark.
gap: Reaches a nuanced verdict (blend, weighted to continuous) not a flat preference.
Higher lexis: standardised, composure under pressure, grade inflation, rigorous benchmark.
Each view is developed with two distinct supports.
Concedes the strengths of exams even while preferring coursework.
Cohesion via concession and contrast (with reason… however… On balance).

prompt: University education should be free for all students. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
University is very expensive in many countries. Some people think it should be free for all students. I agree with this idea.

First, free university gives a chance to poor students. Many clever students cannot study because they have no money. If university is free, everyone can study and this is fair.

Second, the country also gets benefits. More educated people means more doctors, engineers and teachers. This helps the economy to grow.

But some people say free university is too expensive for the government. This is true, but the government can take this money from taxes.

In conclusion, I agree that university should be free because it is fair and good for the country.
band8:
Whether higher education should be free for everyone is a contentious question. While I sympathise with the goal of universal access, I believe wholly free university is neither affordable nor entirely fair, and that targeted support is the wiser path.

The case for free tuition is appealing. It would open universities to talented students from poor backgrounds who are currently deterred by crippling fees, and a more educated workforce benefits the whole economy through innovation and higher productivity.

Yet the policy carries serious drawbacks. Funding free degrees for all, including the wealthy who can readily pay, is enormously expensive and often regressive, since it is financed by taxpayers who never attended university. Such spending might be better directed at schools or vocational training.

A fairer alternative is to means-test support: generous grants and interest-free loans for those who need them, with modest contributions from those who can afford them. In conclusion, I favour widening access through targeted funding rather than blanket free tuition, which I consider both costly and poorly aimed.
gap: Takes a more sophisticated stance (targeted funding, not blanket free) rather than simple agreement.
Economic precision: regressive, means-test, vocational training, productivity.
Acknowledges the strongest counter (access for the poor) and refines rather than dismisses it.
Argues fairness in both directions, showing critical balance.
Controlled concessive structure and varied connectives.

prompt: Distance learning through online courses is becoming popular. Why is this happening? Is it a positive or negative development?
band6:
Nowadays many people study online with distance learning. There are reasons for this and I think it is mostly a positive development.

One reason is technology. Now everyone has internet and computers, so they can study from home easily. Another reason is that people are busy with work and family, so online study is flexible for them.

I think this is mostly positive. Students can study at their own time and they save money on travel. Also, people in villages can study from good universities far away.

But there is one bad point. Students do not meet teachers and friends, so they can feel alone. In conclusion, distance learning is mostly a positive development.
band8:
Online distance learning has grown rapidly in recent years. This trend has clear causes, and although it is not without drawbacks, I regard it as a largely positive development.

Several factors explain its rise. The spread of fast internet and affordable devices has made remote study practical for almost anyone, while the demands of work and family lead many adults to seek flexible courses they can fit around their commitments. The pandemic, too, accelerated acceptance of learning at a distance.

The benefits are substantial. Students can study at their own pace and avoid costly, time-consuming commutes, and learners in remote or underserved areas gain access to institutions that were once geographically out of reach. This democratisation of education is its greatest strength.

The principal weakness is the loss of face-to-face contact, which can leave some students isolated and demands considerable self-discipline. On balance, however, I believe the flexibility and reach of online learning outweigh these costs, provided courses are well designed to keep learners engaged.
gap: Answers both parts (why + evaluation) with a clear, qualified verdict.
Lexis: democratisation of education, underserved areas, self-discipline, accelerated acceptance.
Causes are explained as a connected set, not a flat list.
Identifies the strongest single benefit (reach/access) and weakness (isolation).
Conditional conclusion ('provided courses are well designed').

prompt: Many students choose to study abroad. What are the advantages and disadvantages of studying in a foreign country?
band6:
Many students go to another country to study. This has advantages and disadvantages.

There are some advantages. Students learn about a new culture and they become independent. They also learn a new language and meet people from many countries. This is good for their future job.

There are also disadvantages. Studying abroad is very expensive, so not everyone can do it. Also, students are far from family and they can feel homesick. Sometimes the language is a big problem in classes.

In conclusion, studying abroad has good points like culture and independence, but also bad points like money and homesickness.
band8:
Increasing numbers of students now pursue their degrees overseas, a choice that brings considerable rewards as well as real difficulties.

The advantages are significant. Living in a foreign country immerses students in a new culture and language, accelerating fluency and broadening their outlook in ways a classroom at home rarely can. They also develop independence and resilience, and a foreign qualification, combined with international contacts, can markedly enhance their career prospects.

The drawbacks, however, deserve weight. Studying abroad is often prohibitively expensive once tuition, travel and living costs are combined, placing it beyond many families. Distance from home can breed homesickness and isolation, and the challenge of studying complex material in a second language may, at first, undermine academic performance.

In conclusion, studying abroad offers cultural enrichment, independence and stronger career prospects, but at a high financial and emotional cost. For those who can manage the expense and adjustment, the experience is, in my view, well worth the challenges it presents.
gap: Names the maturity/career benefits precisely and weighs costs concretely.
Stronger lexis: immerses, prohibitively expensive, resilience, broaden one's outlook.
Acknowledges inequality of access (beyond many families).
Evaluative conclusion rather than a flat 'good and bad points'.
Participial and complex structures add grammatical range.

prompt: Individuals should be responsible for sorting their household waste for recycling. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Recycling is important for the environment. Some people think every person should sort their own waste at home. I agree with this.

First, if people sort their waste, recycling is easier and faster. Workers do not have to separate everything, so more things can be recycled. This is good for the planet.

Second, sorting waste makes people think about the environment. When they separate plastic, paper and glass, they understand the problem and they make less waste.

Some people say it takes time and it is boring. But it only takes a few minutes every day. In conclusion, I agree that individuals should sort their household waste.
band8:
As recycling becomes ever more important, many argue that individuals should take responsibility for sorting their own household waste. I largely agree, since such small, collective actions make a meaningful difference, though they cannot succeed in isolation.

The practical benefits are clear. When households separate paper, glass and plastic at source, far less recyclable material is contaminated or sent to landfill, and the whole process becomes cheaper and more efficient. A small effort at the household level, multiplied across a city, yields a substantial environmental return.

Equally important is the change in mindset that sorting encourages. The simple act of dividing one's rubbish makes people conscious of how much they consume and discard, often prompting them to waste less in the first place.

That said, individual effort is only effective when supported by proper infrastructure: clear labelling, accessible collection and facilities that actually process the sorted material. In conclusion, I agree that citizens should sort their waste, provided governments give them the systems that make it worthwhile.
gap: Qualified agreement ('largely agree, though…') with a clear condition.
Lexis: contaminated, at source, collective action, infrastructure.
Adds the systemic point that individual effort needs supporting infrastructure.
Develops the 'mindset' benefit beyond the mechanical one.
Cohesion: Equally important, That said, In conclusion.

prompt: Air pollution in cities is getting worse. What are the causes and what can be done to solve this problem?
band6:
Air pollution in cities is a serious problem today. There are some causes and also some solutions.

There are different causes. The main cause is cars and buses because they produce a lot of smoke. Another cause is factories that burn fuel and make the air dirty. Also, there are too many people in cities now.

There are some solutions too. The government can build more public transport so people use fewer cars. They can also move factories far from the city and use clean energy. People can use bicycles for short trips.

In conclusion, air pollution is caused by cars and factories, but with public transport and clean energy, we can reduce it.
band8:
Air quality in many cities has deteriorated to dangerous levels. The problem springs from several identifiable causes, and a combination of measures could substantially improve the situation.

The causes are chiefly related to combustion. The greatest culprit is road traffic: exhaust from the ever-growing number of vehicles releases harmful particulates and gases. Industry compounds this, as factories burning fossil fuels emit pollutants, while rapid urbanisation concentrates millions of people, and their energy demands, into confined spaces.

Fortunately, the problem is far from insoluble. Governments could invest in clean, reliable public transport and incentivise electric vehicles to cut traffic emissions, and impose strict limits on industrial pollution backed by real enforcement. Promoting cycling, expanding green spaces and shifting to renewable energy would reinforce these gains.

In conclusion, urban air pollution stems mainly from vehicles, industry and dense populations, but through cleaner transport, tighter regulation and a transition to renewable energy, cities can restore far healthier air for their residents.
gap: Causes named precisely (particulates, combustion, urbanisation) not just 'smoke'.
Solutions map directly onto each cause (transport answers traffic, limits answer industry).
Lexis: particulates, incentivise, enforcement, transition to renewables.
'far from insoluble' and 'compounds this' raise the register.
Conclusion synthesises causes and remedies rather than restating.

prompt: Some believe protecting the environment is the responsibility of governments, while others say it is up to individuals. Discuss both views.
band6:
Protecting the environment is very important. Some people think it is the job of the government, but others think individuals must do it. I will discuss both views.

Some people say the government should do it. The government can make laws, control factories and build public transport. They have the power and the money to make big changes.

Other people say individuals are responsible. Each person can recycle, save energy and use less water. If everyone does small things, it makes a big difference.

In my opinion, both must work together. The government makes the rules and people follow them, so the environment can be protected by everyone.
band8:
Responsibility for protecting the environment is often debated, with some placing it on governments and others on individuals. In my view, the two are complementary, and meaningful progress depends on both acting together.

Those who look to the state make a strong case. Only governments can pass and enforce legislation, regulate polluting industries, fund clean infrastructure and negotiate international agreements. These structural levers, beyond the reach of any individual, shape the conditions in which everyone lives.

Others rightly stress personal responsibility. Citizens decide what to consume, how much energy to use and whether to recycle, and their collective choices both reduce harm directly and pressure companies and politicians to act. A population that demands sustainability is itself a powerful force.

Ultimately, neither can succeed alone: government regulation without public cooperation is toothless, while individual effort without supportive policy is overwhelmed. In conclusion, environmental protection is a shared responsibility, most effective when top-down regulation and grassroots action reinforce one another.
gap: Argues complementarity with a clear mechanism, not just 'work together'.
Lexis: structural levers, grassroots action, toothless, sustainability.
Each side developed with concrete powers/choices.
Adds the insight that each fails without the other.
Refined cohesion: Those who look to the state, Others rightly stress, Ultimately.

prompt: The most effective way to encourage people to protect the environment is to increase the price of fuel. Do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think the best way to protect the environment is to make fuel more expensive. I do not fully agree with this idea.

It is true that high fuel prices can help. If petrol is expensive, people drive less and use public transport. This reduces pollution and saves energy.

However, there are problems. High fuel prices hurt poor people the most because they still need to travel to work. Also, the price of food and other things goes up because transport is expensive.

In my opinion, fuel price is not the best way. It is better to build good public transport and use clean energy. In conclusion, I disagree that raising fuel prices is the most effective way.
band8:
It is sometimes argued that raising fuel prices is the single most effective way to make people protect the environment. While higher prices certainly influence behaviour, I disagree that this is the best approach, since it is both blunt and unfair.

There is some logic to the claim. Dearer petrol discourages unnecessary driving, nudging people toward public transport, cycling or more efficient vehicles, and thereby cutting emissions. As a deterrent, price can be powerful.

However, the policy has serious flaws. It is regressive, hitting the poor hardest: low-income families who must commute have little choice but to absorb the cost, while the wealthy barely notice it.
