In the future, space tourism may become common. There are reasons why people want to go to space, and I think it is both positive and negative.

There are some reasons. People are curious and they want to see the Earth from space. It is also an exciting and unique experience that very few people have had. Some rich people want it because it shows their status.

I think this is both positive and negative. The positive side is that it pushes technology forward and inspires people. The negative side is that it is only for rich people and it is bad for the environment because of pollution.

In conclusion, people want space tourism for adventure, and it is both positive and negative.
band8:
Space tourism, once pure science fiction, may become common in the future. People's desire to travel to space is easy to understand, and although the trend has appeal, I view it as, on balance, a questionable development for now.

The motivations are clear. Space offers a once-in-a-lifetime experience and the chance to see Earth from a perspective almost no one has shared, satisfying a deep human curiosity and thirst for adventure. For the very wealthy, it also confers prestige, a status symbol beyond ordinary luxury.

The development is double-edged. On the positive side, demand for space travel drives technological innovation and can inspire interest in science. On the negative side, it remains the preserve of the super-rich, deepening the sense of inequality, and rockets burn enormous amounts of fuel, imposing a heavy environmental cost at a time when emissions urgently need cutting.

In conclusion, people are drawn to space tourism by adventure and prestige. While it may spur innovation, I believe its current environmental cost and exclusivity make it, for the time being, a luxury difficult to justify.
gap: Both parts answered with a weighted, time-qualified verdict.
Lexis: a once-in-a-lifetime experience, confer prestige, the preserve of the super-rich, emissions.
Balances innovation against environmental and equity costs.
Names motives precisely (curiosity, status) rather than vaguely.
Cohesion: The motivations are clear… double-edged… In conclusion.

prompt: Museums and historical sites are mainly visited by tourists, not local people. Why is this? What can be done to attract locals?
band6:
In many countries, museums and historical sites are visited mostly by tourists and not by local people. There are reasons for this, and we can do some things to attract locals.

There are some reasons. First, local people think they can visit any time, so they keep postponing it. Second, the tickets are sometimes expensive for locals. Also, some people find museums boring and not interesting.

We can do some things to attract locals. Museums can offer free entry or discounts for local people. They can also make exhibitions more interactive and fun. Organising special events and activities can also bring more local visitors.

In conclusion, locals do not visit because of habit and price, but free entry and interesting events can attract them.
band8:
In many countries, museums and heritage sites draw mostly tourists rather than residents. There are understandable reasons for this, and a number of measures could encourage more locals to visit.

Several factors explain the pattern. Because such sites are permanently on their doorstep, residents tend to take them for granted, endlessly postponing a visit they assume they can make at any time. Cost is another barrier, as admission can feel expensive for a casual local outing, and some people simply perceive museums as dull, static or irrelevant to their lives.

These obstacles can be overcome. Offering free or discounted entry to residents would remove the financial barrier, while making exhibits more interactive and engaging would dispel the impression of dustiness. Hosting changing exhibitions, evening events, workshops and community activities would give locals fresh reasons to return, and outreach to schools could foster a sense of ownership from an early age.

In conclusion, locals neglect these sites largely out of complacency, cost and perceived dullness. Through free access, lively programming and a genuine sense of relevance, however, museums and historical sites can become valued by their own communities, not just visitors.
gap: Both parts answered, with causes and solutions clearly matched.
Lexis: take it for granted, a sense of ownership, interactive, outreach.
Identifies the psychological 'complacency' cause precisely.
Solutions span price, design, programming and schools.
Cohesion: Several factors… These obstacles can be overcome… In conclusion.

prompt: Some believe globalisation enriches local culture, while others think it erodes it. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Globalisation affects local culture. Some people think it enriches culture, but others think it erodes it. I will discuss both views.

Some people say globalisation enriches culture. People can learn about other countries, try new food and enjoy films and music from around the world. This makes life more interesting and people more open-minded.

Other people say it erodes local culture. Big global brands and one common language spread everywhere, so young people forget their own traditions and languages. Everything becomes the same.

In my opinion, globalisation has both effects, but we can keep our culture if we try. In conclusion, globalisation both enriches and threatens local culture.
band8:
Globalisation's effect on local culture is keenly contested: some see it as enriching, others as corrosive. Having weighed both, I believe it can enrich culture, provided communities make a conscious effort to preserve their own traditions.

Those who welcome globalisation point to a vibrant cross-cultural exchange. People can now savour foreign cuisines, music, films and ideas, broadening their horizons and fostering tolerance, and cultures themselves often blend creatively into something new and richer.

Critics counter that this exchange is dangerously unequal. Powerful global brands and dominant languages spread relentlessly, so that local customs, crafts, festivals and dialects fade as younger generations gravitate toward a homogenised global culture, eroding the distinctiveness that makes each society unique.

In my view, enrichment and erosion are not inevitable opposites. With deliberate support for native languages, arts and traditions, societies can absorb the benefits of global exchange while keeping their identity intact. In conclusion, globalisation both enriches and threatens local culture, and which outcome prevails depends largely on how actively communities choose to defend their heritage.
gap: Reaches a conditional verdict (enrichment if heritage is defended).
Lexis: cross-cultural exchange, homogenised, gravitate toward, distinctiveness.
Both views developed with specific cultural detail.
Argues the two outcomes are not inevitable opposites.
Cohesion: Those who welcome… Critics counter… In my view.

prompt: Traditional festivals and customs are disappearing. Is this a positive or negative development?
band6:
In many countries, traditional festivals and customs are slowly disappearing. I think this is a negative development.

There are reasons why they disappear. People live modern, busy lives and they have no time for old traditions. Young people also prefer modern entertainment like the internet and they are not interested in old customs.

I think this is negative. Traditional festivals are an important part of culture and history. They bring families and communities together and give people a sense of identity. If they disappear, we lose a connection to our past.

In conclusion, the disappearance of traditional festivals is a negative development because we lose our culture and identity.
band8:
In many societies, traditional festivals and customs are gradually fading. While I understand why this is happening, I regard it as a regrettable, negative development.

The reasons for the decline are clear enough. Modern, fast-paced lifestyles leave little time for elaborate traditional observances, and younger generations, drawn to global entertainment and digital culture, often find old customs irrelevant. Urbanisation and migration further weaken the close-knit communities in which such traditions once thrived.

Yet this loss matters. Traditional festivals are far more than quaint rituals; they are living links to a community's history and a powerful source of shared identity. They bring families and neighbours together, transmit values and stories across generations, and lend colour and meaning to the rhythm of the year. As they vanish, a society is left poorer and less rooted, its sense of belonging diminished.

In conclusion, although the erosion of traditions is an understandable consequence of modern life, I consider it a negative development. With effort to revive interest and adapt customs to the present, much of this intangible heritage could still be preserved.
gap: Clear evaluative stance with reasoning, not just assertion.
Lexis: living links, intangible heritage, a sense of belonging, revive interest.
Explains causes (lifestyle, youth, urbanisation) before judging.
Argues the value of festivals beyond nostalgia (identity, transmission).
Cohesion: The reasons… Yet this loss matters… In conclusion.
phrases: a living link to the past

prompt: Human cloning research should be banned. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Human cloning is a controversial topic. Some people think research on it should be banned. I partly agree with this idea.

On one side, cloning research can be dangerous. It raises ethical problems and some people think it is against nature and religion. If it is not controlled, it could be misused.

On the other side, cloning research can have benefits. It can help science to understand diseases and maybe grow organs for sick people. So a complete ban could stop useful discoveries.

In my opinion, cloning should not be banned completely but it should be controlled by strict rules. In conclusion, I partly agree that human cloning research should be banned.
band8:
Human cloning is among the most ethically fraught areas of science, and some argue that research into it should be banned outright. I disagree with a total ban, believing that strict regulation, rather than prohibition, is the wiser course.

The case for banning is not without merit. Reproductive cloning in particular raises profound ethical concerns about identity, consent and the commodification of human life, and many regard it as crossing a moral line. Unregulated, the technology could certainly be misused with disturbing consequences.

However, an outright ban would be a mistake. Therapeutic cloning research holds genuine promise: it could deepen our understanding of disease, advance regenerative medicine and one day grow tissues or organs for transplant, saving countless lives. To forbid all such work would forfeit these potential breakthroughs.

In my view, the answer lies in careful governance, permitting tightly regulated therapeutic research under strict ethical oversight while prohibiting reproductive cloning. In conclusion, rather than banning cloning research altogether, society should regulate it rigorously, distinguishing its dangerous applications from its life-saving ones.
gap: Distinguishes reproductive from therapeutic cloning — a key precision.
Lexis: ethically fraught, regenerative medicine, ethical oversight, cross a moral line.
Refines 'partly agree' into a regulate-not-ban position.
Concedes the ethical danger before arguing the medical promise.
Cohesion: The case for banning… However… In my view.

prompt: Fewer young people are choosing to study science. Why might this be? What can be done to encourage them?
band6:
In many countries, fewer young people choose to study science. There are reasons for this and we can do some things to encourage them.

There are some reasons. First, many students think science is too difficult, especially subjects like physics and chemistry. Second, they think science jobs do not pay well or are boring. So they choose other subjects.

We can do some things to encourage them. Teachers can make science lessons more fun with experiments and real examples. Schools can show students the interesting and well-paid careers in science. Governments can also give scholarships for science students.

In conclusion, young people avoid science because it is hard and seems unattractive, but better teaching and information can encourage them.
band8:
Fewer young people are choosing to study science, a worrying trend with identifiable causes and workable remedies.

The reasons are partly perceptual. Many students regard science, especially physics and chemistry, as forbiddingly difficult, demanding more effort than other subjects for uncertain reward. Others are deterred by a belief that scientific careers are poorly paid, narrow or dull, and uninspiring, exam-driven teaching does little to dispel these impressions.

Several measures could reverse the decline. The most powerful is engaging teaching: hands-on experiments, real-world applications and enthusiastic teachers can reveal how exciting and relevant science truly is. Schools and the media could also showcase the rewarding, well-paid careers that science opens up, from medicine to clean energy, while governments might offer scholarships and invest in laboratories to make the subject more attractive and accessible.

In conclusion, young people are turning away from science largely because they perceive it as hard and unrewarding. Through inspiring teaching, better information about careers and targeted incentives, however, their interest can be rekindled, securing the scientists that society increasingly needs.
gap: Both parts answered with precise causes and matched solutions.
Lexis: perceptual, hands-on, real-world applications, incentives.
Frames the core cause as perception, then targets it.
Solutions span teaching, careers information and government.
Cohesion: The reasons are partly perceptual… Several measures… In conclusion.

prompt: People should be discouraged from using private cars in city centres. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Traffic in city centres is a big problem. Some people think we should discourage people from using private cars there. I agree with this idea.

First, too many cars cause traffic jams and pollution in the city centre. If there are fewer cars, the air will be cleaner and people can move more easily.

Second, city centres can be better for people. Without so many cars, there can be more space for walking, cycling and parks. This makes the city more pleasant and safe.

Of course, people need other ways to travel, so the government must provide good public transport. In conclusion, I agree that private cars should be discouraged in city centres.
band8:
Traffic congestion blights many city centres, and some argue that private cars should be discouraged there. I strongly agree, provided good alternatives are put in place.

The environmental and practical case is compelling. Too many cars crowding into the centre cause chronic congestion and heavy air and noise pollution, harming health and wasting everyone's time. Reducing private traffic, through congestion charges, pedestrian zones and limited parking, would cut emissions and let the remaining traffic flow more freely.

Discouraging cars also transforms the centre for people. Space once given over to vehicles can be reclaimed for pedestrians, cyclists, cafes and green areas, making the city safer, quieter and far more pleasant to inhabit. Many cities that have done this have seen their centres flourish.

Crucially, however, such measures only work if reliable alternatives exist. People must be able to reach the centre easily by frequent, affordable public transport, cycling and walking. In conclusion, I firmly support discouraging private cars in city centres, since, paired with good public transport, it makes urban life cleaner, safer and more liveable.
gap: Firm stance with a clear condition (good alternatives).
Lexis: congestion charges, pedestrian zones, reclaim space, liveable.
Covers both pollution and the human transformation of the centre.
Insists alternatives are a precondition, not an afterthought.
Cohesion: The case is compelling… also transforms… Crucially however.

prompt: Road accidents are a serious problem in many countries. What are the causes and what measures can reduce them?
band6:
Road accidents are a serious problem in many countries. There are causes for this and also some measures to reduce them.

There are some causes. The main cause is dangerous driving, like speeding and using a phone while driving. Another cause is bad roads with holes and no lights. Also, some drivers do not follow the traffic rules.

There are some measures. The government can make stricter rules and bigger fines for dangerous drivers. The police can control the roads better. Roads should also be repaired and improved. Drivers should get good training before they get a licence.

In conclusion, road accidents are caused by dangerous driving and bad roads, but stricter rules and better roads can reduce them.
band8:
Road accidents claim countless lives in many countries, a tragedy with clear causes and preventable through targeted measures.

The causes are largely human and infrastructural. The greatest is reckless driving, speeding, drink-driving and the growing menace of using a phone at the wheel, all of which dramatically raise the risk of a crash. Poor road conditions compound the danger, with potholes, inadequate lighting and missing signs catching drivers out, while a general disregard for traffic rules and insufficient driver training make matters worse.

A combination of measures could sharply reduce the toll. Stricter penalties for dangerous driving, backed by rigorous police enforcement and speed cameras, would deter the worst behaviour. Investing in well-maintained, well-lit roads with clear signage would remove many hazards, and demanding thorough driver education before licences are granted would produce safer drivers. Public-awareness campaigns can reinforce the message.

In conclusion, road accidents stem chiefly from reckless driving and poor infrastructure. Through firm enforcement, better roads and proper driver training, however, the number of casualties can be cut significantly.
gap: Causes named precisely (reckless driving, infrastructure) and categorised.
Lexis: reckless driving, enforcement, signage, driver education.
Solutions map directly onto each cause.
'the growing menace of using a phone' adds specificity and register.
Strong synthesising conclusion.

prompt: Some think investment should go into public transport, while others prefer building roads. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
When there is traffic, some people think the government should invest in public transport, but others prefer building more roads. I will discuss both views.

Some people prefer building roads. They say more roads give more space for cars, so traffic moves faster. New roads also connect places and help business.

Other people prefer public transport. They say buses and trains can carry many people, so there are fewer cars on the road. This reduces traffic and pollution.

In my opinion, public transport is the better choice because more roads just bring more cars. In conclusion, I think investing in public transport is better than building roads.
band8:
Faced with worsening traffic, governments must decide whether to invest in public transport or build more roads. Having weighed both approaches, I firmly favour public transport, since simply adding roads tends to be self-defeating.

The case for road-building seems intuitive. More lanes promise to ease bottlenecks, shorten journeys and improve the movement of goods, and in genuinely under-served or rural areas new connections can bring real benefits.

However, the evidence favours public transport. Because of "induced demand", new roads soon fill with extra cars and the congestion returns, whereas efficient buses, trains and trams move large numbers of people in far less space, easing jams and cutting emissions at once. Good public transport also serves those without cars and reduces a city's dependence on the private vehicle.

In my view, the durable solution is to invest in reliable, affordable public transport, supplemented by cycling and walking networks, rather than chasing congestion with ever more tarmac. In conclusion, while new roads have their place, I believe public transport is the wiser investment, addressing traffic at its source rather than feeding it.
gap: Names 'induced demand' — an evidence-based concept.
Lexis: bottlenecks, car dependency, durable solution, induced demand.
Concedes the road-building case before rebutting with evidence.
Notes equity (serves those without cars).
Cohesion: The case seems intuitive… However… In my view.

prompt: People today are more selfish and less willing to help others than in the past. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think that people today are more selfish and help others less than in the past. I partly agree with this idea.

On one side, modern life is very busy and people focus on their own work and family. In big cities, people often do not even know their neighbours, so they help others less.

On the other side, many people are still kind. They donate to charity, do volunteer work and help during disasters. The internet also helps people support good causes quickly.

In my opinion, society has changed but people are not really more selfish. In conclusion, I partly agree, but I think kindness still exists today.
band8:
It is often claimed that people today are more selfish and less willing to help others than in the past. While modern life has weakened some bonds, I do not accept that people have genuinely become more selfish.

There is some basis for the concern. The pace and pressure of modern life lead many to focus narrowly on their own work and families, and the anonymity of large cities, where people rarely know their neighbours, can erode the everyday mutual help that smaller communities once took for granted.

Nevertheless, to conclude that selfishness has triumphed ignores abundant evidence of generosity. People continue to donate to charity, volunteer their time and respond with remarkable solidarity to disasters, and technology now lets compassion mobilise across the globe within hours. Kindness has not disappeared; it has often simply changed its form.

In my view, what has shifted is the structure of community, not the human capacity for altruism. In conclusion, I partly accept that modern life can make people seem less neighbourly, but I believe generosity remains very much alive, expressed in new ways rather than lost.
gap: Reframes the claim (structure changed, not human nature) — a sharp insight.
Lexis: anonymity, solidarity, altruism, mobilise compassion.
Concedes the weakening of local bonds before rebutting.
Cites concrete evidence of continued generosity.
Cohesion: There is some basis… Nevertheless… In my view.

prompt: In many countries the gap between rich and poor is widening. Why is this happening? What can be done to reduce it?
band6:
In many countries, the gap between rich and poor is becoming bigger. There are reasons for this and also some solutions.

There are some reasons. First, rich people have good education and good jobs, so they earn more and more. Poor people have less education and low-paid jobs. Second, the system sometimes helps the rich, for example with low taxes for big companies.

There are some solutions. The government can make taxes fairer, so rich people pay more. They can also spend money on free education and health for poor people. Creating good jobs also helps to reduce the gap.

In conclusion, the gap grows because of unequal education and jobs, but fair taxes and education can reduce it.
band8:
In many countries the gulf between rich and poor is widening, a trend with deep causes and real, if difficult, solutions.

The causes are structural. Wealth tends to compound: the affluent can afford superior education, secure high-paying work and invest their savings, while the poor, with limited education and access only to low-wage jobs, struggle to advance. Globalisation and automation have rewarded skilled, capital-owning workers while squeezing the unskilled, and tax systems that favour the wealthy often entrench these advantages.

Several measures could narrow the divide. Fairer, more progressive taxation, ensuring the rich contribute their proper share, can fund investment in free, high-quality education and healthcare that boosts social mobility for the disadvantaged. A robust safety net, a decent minimum wage and the creation of good jobs would further help those at the bottom, while cracking down on tax avoidance would shore up public finances.

In conclusion, inequality is widening mainly because of unequal opportunity and an uneven economic system. Through progressive taxation, investment in opportunity and stronger protection for the poor, however, the gap can be meaningfully reduced.
gap: Both parts answered with structural causes and matched solutions.
Lexis: wealth compounds, social mobility, progressive taxation, safety net.
Explains why inequality is self-reinforcing.
Solutions are specific and causally linked.
Cohesion: The causes are structural… Several measures… In conclusion.

prompt: Some think volunteering should be compulsory for young people, while others disagree. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Some people think volunteering should be compulsory for young people, while others disagree. I will discuss both views.

Some people support compulsory volunteering. They say it teaches young people responsibility and useful skills. It also helps the community and makes young people understand the problems of other people.

Other people disagree. They say if volunteering is forced, it is not real volunteering. Young people may do it badly because they do not want to. Also, they are busy with study and have no time.

In my opinion, volunteering should be encouraged but not forced. In conclusion, both views have value, but I think it should not be compulsory.
band8:
Whether young people should be required to volunteer divides opinion. Having considered both sides, I believe volunteering should be actively encouraged rather than made compulsory.

Supporters of compulsion argue that it instils a sense of civic duty and responsibility, equips young people with valuable experience and skills, and exposes them to lives different from their own, fostering empathy. The community, meanwhile, gains willing hands for worthy causes.

Opponents, however, raise a telling objection: compulsion arguably defeats the very purpose of volunteering, which is freely given help. Forced participation may breed resentment and half-hearted effort, and adding obligations to already busy students could crowd out their studies. Genuine commitment cannot easily be mandated.

In my view, the better path is to inspire rather than compel, schools can promote volunteering enthusiastically, celebrate it and make opportunities easily available, so that young people choose to take part. In conclusion, while compulsory volunteering has admirable aims, I believe it is self-contradictory; encouraging voluntary service willingly given achieves the same goals more authentically.
gap: Clear stance (encourage, not compel) with a principled reason.
Lexis: civic duty, defeat the purpose, genuine commitment, mandate.
Identifies the core paradox of 'forced volunteering'.
Each view developed with distinct points.
Cohesion: Supporters argue… Opponents however… In my view.

prompt: People today buy far more than they need. To what extent do you agree, and what problems does this cause?
band6:
Today people buy far more things than they need. I agree with this, and it causes some problems.

It is true that people buy too much. Advertising and easy shopping make people want new things all the time, like new phones and clothes, even when the old ones are fine.

This causes some problems. First, people waste a lot of money and some get into debt. Second, it is bad for the environment because more production and more rubbish cause pollution. Also, people are never satisfied and always want more.

In conclusion, I agree that people buy more than they need, and this causes money problems and environmental damage.
band8:
It is undeniable that people today buy far more than they need, and I agree this over-consumption brings serious problems, both personal and environmental.

That modern life encourages excessive buying is clear. Relentless advertising, easy credit and the convenience of online shopping fuel a throwaway culture in which people constantly replace perfectly usable phones, clothes and gadgets simply to keep up with trends. Possessions have become a measure of status rather than a response to need.

This excess causes real harm. Financially, many people overspend and slide into mounting debt, chasing a satisfaction that fresh purchases never deliver. Environmentally, the consequences are graver still: producing, transporting and disposing of all these goods consumes vast resources and generates mountains of waste, accelerating pollution and climate change. Psychologically, too, a culture of endless wanting leaves people perpetually dissatisfied.

In conclusion, I firmly agree that people buy more than they need, and this over-consumption damages personal finances, the environment and even our contentment. Living more simply, within our means, would benefit individuals and the planet alike.
gap: Strong agreement with problems organised (financial, environmental, psychological).
Lexis: a throwaway culture, mounting debt, over-consumption, within our means.
Explains the cause (advertising, credit, status) before the harms.
Adds the psychological 'endless wanting' dimension.
Cohesion: That modern life… This excess… In conclusion.

prompt: Advertising encourages people to buy things they do not need. Why is advertising so powerful? What can be done about it?
band6:
Advertising is very powerful and it makes people buy things they do not need. There are reasons why it is so strong, and we can do some things about it.

There are some reasons. Advertising is everywhere, on TV, the internet and the street, so people see it all the time. It also uses clever tricks, like beautiful images and famous people, to make products attractive and to create desire.

We can do some things about it. The government can make rules to stop false and misleading adverts. Schools can teach people, especially children, to think carefully and not believe everything they see. People can also make a shopping list and buy only what they need.

In conclusion, advertising is powerful because it is everywhere and clever, but rules and education can reduce its effect.
band8:
Advertising wields enormous influence, persuading people to buy goods they neither need nor want. Its power has clear sources, and several measures could blunt its effect.

Advertising is potent for two main reasons. First, it is utterly pervasive, saturating television, the internet, social media and public space, so that we absorb thousands of messages daily, often unconsciously. Second, it is psychologically sophisticated: by associating products with happiness, beauty, status or belonging, and by deploying celebrities and emotive imagery, it creates artificial desires and plays on our deepest insecurities.

Its grip can nonetheless be loosened. Governments can regulate advertising, banning misleading claims and restricting adverts aimed at children, who are least able to resist them. Teaching media literacy in schools would equip people to recognise and question persuasive techniques, while individuals can protect themselves by shopping deliberately, from a list, rather than on impulse.

In conclusion, advertising is so powerful because it is everywhere and expertly exploits human psychology. Through sensible regulation, media literacy and conscious consumer habits, however, its influence over what we buy can be meaningfully curbed.
gap: Both parts answered, with two precise reasons for advertising's power.
Lexis: pervasive, artificial desires, media literacy, on impulse.
Explains the psychological mechanism, not just 'clever tricks'.
Solutions span regulation, education and personal habit.
Cohesion: Advertising is potent for two reasons… Its grip… In conclusion.

prompt: Children should learn a foreign language from primary school. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think children should start learning a foreign language in primary school. I agree with this idea.

First, young children learn languages very easily. Their brains can absorb new sounds and words quickly, so they can become fluent more easily than adults.

Second, knowing a foreign language is very useful today. In a global world, it helps with travel, study and future jobs. Children who learn early have an advantage.

Some people say it can confuse young children or take time from other subjects. But research shows children can learn two languages well. In conclusion, I agree that children should learn a foreign language from primary school.
band8:
Whether children should begin a foreign language in primary school is much debated. I strongly agree, since the early years are uniquely suited to language learning and the benefits are considerable.

The most compelling reason is developmental. Young children are in a "critical period" for language acquisition, absorbing new sounds, vocabulary and grammar with a natural ease that adults struggle to match. Starting early therefore makes genuine fluency far more attainable.

The practical advantages are equally strong. In an interconnected world, a second language is a valuable asset for travel, study and employment, and children who acquire one young enjoy a lasting head start. Learning another language also brings cognitive benefits, sharpening memory and broadening cultural understanding.

Critics worry that early language teaching may confuse children or crowd out core subjects. The evidence, however, suggests the opposite: young learners handle two languages comfortably, and the cognitive gains often aid other learning. In conclusion, I firmly believe children should learn a foreign language from primary school, since they learn it best then and reap benefits that last a lifetime.
gap: Firm stance grounded in the 'critical period' concept.
Lexis: language acquisition, cognitive benefits, a head start, fluency.
Develops developmental and practical arguments distinctly.
Concedes and rebuts the 'confusion' objection with evidence.
Cohesion: The most compelling reason… equally strong… Critics worry.

prompt: Some think English should be the only global language, while others value linguistic diversity. Discuss both views.
band6:
Some people think English should be the only global language, but others think we should protect language diversity. I will discuss both views.

Some people support one global language. They say if everyone speaks English, communication is easy. Business, travel and study become simpler and there are fewer misunderstandings between countries.

Other people want diversity. They say each language is part of a culture and identity. If everyone speaks only English, many small languages will die and the world will lose its cultural richness.

In my opinion, English is useful as a common language, but we must also protect other languages. In conclusion, both views are valuable, but I think diversity should be protected.
band8:
Some argue that English should become the world's only global language, while others insist on protecting linguistic diversity. Having weighed both, I believe a shared language and diversity can, and should, coexist.

Supporters of a single global tongue stress convenience. If everyone spoke English, international trade, science, travel and diplomacy would run more smoothly, and the misunderstandings that language barriers cause would largely disappear. A common medium genuinely binds the world together.

Defenders of diversity, however, raise a profound concern. Languages are not mere tools but vessels of culture, memory and identity; were English to dominate entirely, thousands of smaller languages would face extinction, and with each one lost would go a unique way of seeing the world. Such homogenisation would impoverish humanity's heritage.

In my view, the goal should not be one language replacing all others, but bilingualism: embracing English as a practical lingua franca while actively preserving native tongues. In conclusion, although a common language brings real benefits, I believe linguistic diversity must be protected, for a world that speaks only one language would be the poorer for it.
gap: Reaches a synthesis (coexistence/bilingualism), not a flat both-views.
Lexis: a common medium, vessels of culture, lingua franca, homogenisation.
The 'languages as vessels of culture' argument adds depth.
Both views developed with concrete consequences.
Cohesion: Supporters stress… Defenders however… In my view.

prompt: Schools should teach practical skills such as cooking and budgeting rather than only academic subjects. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think schools should teach practical skills like cooking and budgeting instead of only academic subjects. I partly agree with this idea.

On one side, practical skills are very useful for life. Everyone needs to cook, manage money and look after a home. If schools teach these, students are ready for real life.

On the other side, academic subjects are also important. Subjects like maths, science and languages are necessary for university and many careers. Schools should not remove them.

In my opinion, schools should teach practical skills but also keep academic subjects. In conclusion, I partly agree, and I think a balance of both is the best.
band8:
Some maintain that schools should teach practical skills such as cooking and budgeting rather than concentrating on academic subjects. While I see the value of life skills, I disagree that they should replace academic learning; both deserve a place.

There is a strong case for practical instruction. Everyone, whatever their career, must eventually cook, manage money and run a household, yet many young people leave school unable to do these basic things. Teaching such skills would prepare students for the realities of adult life and give school clear real-world relevance.

However, to push academic subjects aside would be a serious mistake. Mathematics, science, languages and the humanities develop the knowledge and critical thinking on which higher education and most professions depend, and abandoning them would limit students' future options.

In my view, this is not an either-or choice. A well-rounded education can include practical life skills, perhaps through dedicated lessons, without sacrificing academic rigour. In conclusion, I believe schools should teach practical skills alongside, not instead of, academic subjects, equipping students for both work and everyday life.
gap: Refines 'partly agree' into 'alongside, not instead of'.
Lexis: real-world relevance, critical thinking, well-rounded, rigour.
Each side developed with specific examples.
Rejects the false either-or framing of the prompt.
Cohesion: There is a strong case… However… In my view.

prompt: Many people do not get enough sleep. Why is this a problem, and what can be done to address it?
band6:
Many people today do not get enough sleep. This is a problem and there are some solutions.

Sleep problems are serious. When people do not sleep enough, they cannot concentrate at work or school and they make more mistakes. In the long term, lack of sleep can also cause health problems like stress and heart disease.

There are some reasons and solutions. One reason is using phones and computers late at night. People should stop using screens before bed. Another reason is stress and busy lives. People should have a regular sleep routine and go to bed at the same time.

In conclusion, lack of sleep is a problem because it harms health and work, but good habits can solve it.
band8:
A great many people today fail to get enough sleep, a deprivation that causes real harm but can be addressed through changes in habit.

The consequences of insufficient sleep are serious. In the short term, it impairs concentration, memory and mood, leading to poorer performance at work or school and a greater risk of accidents. Over the long term, chronic sleep deprivation is linked to weakened immunity, anxiety, obesity and heart disease, making it a genuine threat to health.

Both the causes and the remedies are clear. A major culprit is screen use late at night, as the light from phones and laptops disrupts the body's natural rhythms; switching devices off well before bed would help considerably. Stress and disorganised, over-busy lifestyles also rob people of rest, so establishing a regular routine, going to bed and rising at consistent times, and prioritising sleep rather than sacrificing it would make a marked difference.

In conclusion, inadequate sleep harms both health and daily performance, but through better screen habits, a steady routine and a genuine commitment to rest, most people could sleep far better than they do.
gap: Both parts answered, separating short- and long-term harms.
Lexis: sleep deprivation, impair concentration, natural rhythms, routine.
Causes (screens, stress) paired with matched solutions.
'rob people of rest' and 'a genuine threat to health' raise register.
Cohesion: The consequences… Both the causes and remedies… In conclusion.

prompt: Working from home is better than working in an office. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Today many people work from home instead of in an office. Some people think working from home is better. I partly agree with this idea.

Working from home has advantages. People save time and money because they do not travel to the office. They also have more flexibility and can spend time with their family.

However, the office also has advantages. In the office, people can work together easily and share ideas. They also meet colleagues and do not feel lonely. At home, there are many distractions.

In my opinion, it depends on the person and the job. In conclusion, I partly agree that working from home is better, but the office also has benefits.
band8:
Remote working has become widespread, and many now consider it better than working in an office. In my view, neither is simply superior; each suits different people and roles.

Home working offers genuine benefits. Eliminating the daily commute saves time, money and stress, while the flexibility it brings allows a healthier balance between work and family life. Free from a noisy office, some people also concentrate better and become more productive.

The office, however, retains real advantages. It enables the spontaneous collaboration, quick discussions and mentoring that drive teamwork and creativity, and it provides social contact that wards off the isolation many remote workers feel. The home, by contrast, is full of distractions and can blur the line between work and rest.

In my view, the better option depends on the individual and the nature of the job: solitary, focused work may flourish at home, while collaborative roles benefit from the office. A hybrid arrangement often captures the best of both. In conclusion, I do not believe home working is universally better; the ideal balance varies from person to person.
gap: Reframes to 'neither universally superior', a sharper stance than 'partly'.
Lexis: spontaneous collaboration, work-life balance, isolation, hybrid.
Both settings developed with distinct, concrete advantages.
Ties the verdict to job type and proposes a hybrid resolution.
Cohesion: Home working offers… The office however… In my view.

prompt: Some think nuclear power is the answer to energy needs, while others consider it too dangerous. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Some people think nuclear power is the answer to our energy needs, while others think it is too dangerous. I will discuss both views.

Some people support nuclear power. They say it produces a lot of energy and very little carbon, so it is good for climate change. It can also meet the rising demand for electricity.

Other people think it is dangerous. They worry about accidents like Chernobyl and Fukushima, which were terrible disasters. There is also the problem of radioactive waste that stays dangerous for a long time.

In my opinion, nuclear power has benefits but also risks, so it must be used very carefully. In conclusion, both views are valid, but safety is the most important thing.
band8:
Nuclear power divides opinion sharply: some hail it as the solution to our energy needs, others condemn it as too dangerous. Weighing both views, I believe nuclear power has an important, if carefully managed, role to play.

Its supporters present a strong case. Nuclear plants generate vast amounts of reliable electricity while emitting almost no carbon, making them a powerful weapon against climate change, and they can meet rising demand far more consistently than weather-dependent renewables alone.

Critics, however, point to undeniable dangers. The catastrophic accidents at Chernobyl and Fukushima showed how devastating a failure can be, and the radioactive waste produced remains hazardous for thousands of years, posing a problem no one has fully solved. Plants are also hugely expensive and slow to build.

In my view, these risks call for caution rather than outright rejection. With rigorous safety standards, modern reactor designs and responsible waste management, nuclear power can be a valuable low-carbon source alongside renewables. In conclusion, while the dangers are real, I believe well-regulated nuclear energy is, on balance, a necessary part of a sustainable energy future.
gap: Reaches a clear, conditional verdict (a role if well-managed).
Lexis: low-carbon, radioactive waste, reactor designs, weather-dependent.
Both views developed with concrete, named evidence.
Frames safety as the decisive condition, not an afterthought.
Cohesion: Its supporters… Critics however… In my view.

prompt: Social media has made people less able to communicate face to face. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think social media has made people less able to talk face to face. I partly agree with this idea.

On one side, it is true that many people, especially young people, spend a lot of time on social media. They prefer to chat online and they feel shy or uncomfortable in real conversations. So their social skills become weaker.

On the other side, social media also helps communication. People can stay in touch with friends and family who are far away, and they can meet new people with the same interests.

In my opinion, the problem is not social media itself but how people use it. In conclusion, I partly agree that social media weakens face-to-face communication.
band8:
It is frequently argued that social media has eroded people's ability to communicate face to face. While I accept there is some truth in this, I believe the problem lies in how the technology is used rather than in the technology itself.

There is reason for concern. Many people, especially the young, now spend hours communicating through screens, and some grow so accustomed to hiding behind a keyboard that they feel awkward or anxious in real conversation. Over-reliance on texting can leave social skills, reading body language, sustaining a dialogue, underdeveloped.

Yet social media also has communicative value. It allows people to stay connected with distant friends and family and to meet others who share their interests, and for the shy it can even be a gentler way to build relationships. Used in moderation, it complements rather than replaces face-to-face contact.

In my view, then, the issue is balance. Those who let online interaction crowd out real conversation may indeed lose fluency, but the fault is one of habit. In conclusion, I partly agree that social media can weaken face-to-face communication, though mindful use need not.
gap: Locates the issue in usage, not the technology — a nuanced thesis.
Lexis: hide behind a keyboard, read body language, in moderation, mindful use.
Concedes the skills concern before presenting the upside.
Frames the verdict around balance/habit.
Cohesion: There is reason for concern… Yet… In my view.

prompt: In some countries, people are choosing to have fewer children. Why is this happening? What effects might it have?
band6:
In some countries, people are choosing to have fewer children. There are reasons for this and it has some effects.

There are some reasons. First, raising children is very expensive today, so people cannot afford many. Second, many women now focus on their careers, so they have children later or have fewer.

This has some effects. One effect is that the population gets older, with many old people and few young people. Another effect is that in the future there will be fewer workers, so the economy can have problems.

In conclusion, people have fewer children because of cost and careers, and this leads to an ageing population and fewer workers.
band8:
In a number of countries, people are increasingly choosing to have fewer children. This trend has clear causes and significant long-term effects.

The reasons are largely economic and social. Raising a child has become extremely expensive, encompassing housing, education and childcare, so many couples feel they can afford only one or two. Changing priorities also play a part: more women now pursue careers and personal goals, delaying parenthood or opting for smaller families, while greater access to contraception makes such choices possible.

The consequences are far-reaching. The most immediate is an ageing population, in which a growing proportion of elderly citizens is supported by a shrinking number of young people. This threatens to strain pension and healthcare systems and to produce labour shortages, as too few workers enter the economy to sustain growth and care for the old.

In conclusion, falling birth rates stem chiefly from the high cost of children and shifting priorities, and they point toward an ageing society with a smaller workforce. Governments may need to support young families and adapt their economies to manage these effects.
gap: Both parts answered, with causes and downstream effects.
Lexis: ageing population, labour shortages, shrinking workforce, contraception.
Explains why the demographic shift causes economic strain.
Adds a forward-looking policy note in the conclusion.
Cohesion: The reasons are… The consequences… In conclusion.

prompt: Teachers are more important than the resources a school has. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think teachers are more important than the resources a school has. I agree with this idea.

First, a good teacher can inspire students and make them love learning. Even with few resources, a passionate teacher can explain well and motivate students to do their best.

Second, resources alone cannot teach. A school can have computers and books, but without good teachers students will not learn well. The teacher guides and supports the students every day.

Of course, good resources are also helpful. But in my opinion the teacher is more important. In conclusion, I agree that teachers matter more than the resources a school has.
band8:
It is often claimed that teachers matter more than a school's resources. I largely agree, since the human element of education proves decisive, even though good resources clearly help.

The strongest argument concerns inspiration. A gifted, dedicated teacher can ignite curiosity, explain difficult ideas clearly and motivate students to fulfil their potential, often achieving remarkable results with very little. Many successful people credit a single inspiring teacher, not lavish facilities, for their achievements.

Moreover, resources cannot teach themselves. A school may possess computers, laboratories and well-stocked libraries, but without skilled teachers to guide their use, this equipment yields little. It is the teacher who diagnoses difficulties, adapts to each learner and sustains the daily encouragement that genuine learning requires.

This is not to dismiss resources, which can certainly enrich teaching and widen opportunity. On balance, however, I believe the teacher remains the more important factor: excellent teaching can compensate for modest facilities, whereas the finest facilities cannot compensate for poor teaching. In conclusion, I agree that teachers outweigh resources, because it is they who bring learning to life.
gap: Qualified agreement that still acknowledges resources.
Lexis: the human element, ignite curiosity, compensate for, fulfil potential.
Develops inspiration and the 'resources can't teach themselves' lines.
Memorable contrast: teaching can offset facilities, not vice versa.
Cohesion: The strongest argument… Moreover… On balance.
phrases: make the most of limited resources

prompt: Some think governments should provide free healthcare for all, while others believe people should pay. Discuss both views.
band6:
Some people think the government should provide free healthcare for everyone, while others believe people should pay for it. I will discuss both views.

Some people support free healthcare. They say health is a basic right and everyone should get treatment, especially poor people who cannot pay. Free healthcare keeps the whole society healthy.

Other people think people should pay. They say free healthcare is very expensive for the government and people may waste it. If people pay, they use the service more carefully.

In my opinion, healthcare should be free or at least very cheap for everyone, especially the poor. In conclusion, both views have points, but I support free healthcare.
band8:
Whether governments should provide free healthcare or expect people to pay is a fundamental debate. Having weighed both views, I firmly support universal access to healthcare, free at the point of use for those who cannot afford it.

Advocates of free healthcare make a powerful moral case. Health is a basic human right, and no one should be denied treatment or ruined financially because they fall ill. A system funded through taxation ensures that the poor and vulnerable are cared for, and a healthier population benefits the whole of society and economy.

Those who favour payment argue that free services are enormously costly and may be overused or wasted, whereas charging encourages people to value care and use it responsibly. There is some practical force in this concern about sustainability.

In my view, however, the moral imperative outweighs it. The fairest model funds healthcare publicly while managing demand sensibly, so that no one is left behind. In conclusion, although cost is a genuine challenge, I believe healthcare should be a universal right rather than a privilege reserved for those who can pay.
gap: Clear stance (universal access) reached after weighing both.
Lexis: a basic human right, free at the point of use, sustainability, taxation.
Concedes the cost/overuse argument with 'some practical force'.
Distinguishes a right from a privilege.
Cohesion: Advocates make… Those who favour… In my view.

prompt: Foreign tourists should pay more than local visitors to see a country's attractions. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
In some countries, foreign tourists pay more than local people to visit attractions. Some people think this is fair, but I do not fully agree with this idea.

On one side, there are good reasons for this. Local people pay taxes that support these places, so it is fair they pay less. Foreign tourists usually have more money and the extra money can help to protect the attractions.

On the other side, it can seem unfair. All visitors enjoy the same place, so why should they pay different prices? It can also give a bad impression to tourists.

In my opinion, a small difference is acceptable but a big difference is not fair. In conclusion, I partly agree with charging foreign tourists more.
band8:
In some countries, foreign tourists are charged more than locals to enter attractions, a practice some defend as fair. I partly agree: a modest difference is reasonable, but a large one is hard to justify.

There are sound arguments for the policy. Local residents already fund the upkeep of these sites through their taxes, so it seems fair that they should pay less, while foreign visitors, who generally have greater spending power, can readily absorb a higher charge. The extra revenue can also support the conservation of the very heritage tourists come to see, a genuine public benefit.

However, the practice has drawbacks. Charging different prices for the same experience can strike visitors as discriminatory and unwelcoming, potentially harming a country's reputation and deterring tourism. A steep premium, in particular, may feel like exploitation.

In my view, the key is proportion. A small, reasonable surcharge that funds maintenance is acceptable and even sensible, whereas a large markup is unfair and counterproductive. In conclusion, I partly support charging foreign tourists more, provided the difference is modest and the revenue genuinely benefits the attractions.
gap: Qualified verdict turning on proportion (modest yes, steep no).
Lexis: spending power, conservation, surcharge, counterproductive.
Both sides developed with concrete reasoning.
Adds the reputational/tourism-deterrence risk.
Cohesion: There are sound arguments… However… In my view.

prompt: Many tasks once done by people are now done by machines. Why is this happening? Is it a positive or negative development?
band6:
Today many tasks that people used to do are now done by machines. There are reasons for this and I think it is both positive and negative.

There are some reasons. Machines are faster and they do not get tired, so they increase production. They are also cheaper for companies in the long term because they do not need a salary.

I think this is both positive and negative. The positive side is that machines do dangerous and boring work, and products become cheaper. The negative side is that many workers lose their jobs and they cannot easily find new ones.

In conclusion, machines replace people because they are fast and cheap, and this development is both positive and negative.
band8:
Increasingly, tasks once performed by people are carried out by machines. This automation has clear causes, and I regard it as a broadly positive development, despite real disruption.

The reasons are economic and technological. Machines work faster, more precisely and without rest, raising productivity, and although costly to install, they are cheaper than human labour over time, since they require no wages or breaks. Advances in robotics and artificial intelligence have simply made them capable of ever more tasks.

The consequences are genuinely mixed. On the positive side, machines relieve people of dangerous, repetitive or exhausting work, lower the cost of goods and free human effort for more creative and rewarding roles. On the negative side, automation displaces many workers, and those who lack the skills to move into new occupations may face lasting unemployment and hardship.

In conclusion, machines are replacing human labour because they are faster, cheaper and ever more capable. Provided societies invest in retraining and help workers transition into the new jobs automation creates, I believe this development is, on balance, a positive one.
gap: Both parts answered with a weighted, conditional verdict.
Lexis: automation, productivity, displace workers, transition.
Causes explained (speed, cost, AI advances) before effects.
Adds the retraining condition that makes the trend positive.
Cohesion: The reasons are… The consequences… In conclusion.

prompt: Watching films is a better way to learn about a country than reading books. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think watching films is a better way to learn about a country than reading books. I do not fully agree with this idea.

It is true that films have advantages. They show the real places, people, clothes and how people live, so it is easy and interesting to understand a country. Films also do not take much time.

However, books are also very good. They explain history, ideas and feelings in more depth than films. A film is short and it can only show a small part, but a book can give detailed knowledge.

In my opinion, films and books are both useful and they work best together. In conclusion, I do not fully agree that films are better than books for learning about a country.
band8:
Some maintain that watching films teaches us more about a country than reading books. While films have real strengths, I disagree that they are simply better; the two are best regarded as complementary.

Films certainly have advantages. They are vivid and immersive, bringing a country's landscapes, faces, fashions and daily life to the screen in a way words alone cannot, and they convey atmosphere and emotion instantly and accessibly. For a quick, engaging impression, film is hard to beat.

Books, however, offer a depth that film struggles to match. Through detailed description and analysis, they can explore a nation's history, politics, values and inner life far more thoroughly than a two-hour film, which can only capture a fragment. Reading also demands active imagination, fostering a more reflective understanding.

In my view, neither medium is inherently superior; films supply immediacy and colour, books supply depth and nuance, and together they give the fullest picture. In conclusion, I do not accept that films are better than books for learning about a country, since each reveals what the other leaves out.
gap: Reframes to 'complementary', rejecting the simple comparison.
Lexis: immersive, atmosphere, depth and nuance, a fragment.
Both media developed with distinct strengths.
Argues each reveals what the other omits.
Cohesion: Films certainly… Books however… In my view.

prompt: Some believe homework is essential, while others think it places too much pressure on children. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Some people think homework is essential, while others think it puts too much pressure on children. I will discuss both views.

Some people support homework. They say it helps students review and remember what they learned in class. It also teaches them to study alone and to be responsible.

Other people are against too much homework. They say children study all day and then they have no time to play, rest or be with family. Too much homework causes stress and tiredness.

In my opinion, homework is useful but it should not be too much. In conclusion, both views are valid, and I think a reasonable amount of homework is the best.
band8:
Homework divides parents and educators: some consider it essential, others a source of undue pressure on children. Having weighed both views, I believe homework is valuable in moderation but harmful in excess.

Those who defend homework argue, reasonably, that it reinforces classroom learning, helping pupils consolidate and remember what they have been taught. It also cultivates independent study habits, time-management and a sense of responsibility that serve students well in later life.

Critics, however, warn of the dangers of too much. Children who study all day and then face hours of homework are left with little time to play, rest or spend with their families, and the resulting fatigue and stress can damage both wellbeing and motivation. Excessive homework may even make learning a chore rather than a pleasure.

In my view, the answer lies in quality and quantity. A modest amount of meaningful, well-designed homework reinforces learning without overwhelming children, whereas heavy, repetitive workloads do more harm than good. In conclusion, homework is beneficial when reasonable, but schools should guard against the excess that turns a useful tool into a burden.
gap: Reaches a clear verdict (useful in moderation, harmful in excess).
Lexis: consolidate, independent study, fatigue, quality over quantity.
Each view developed with distinct supports.
Notes that excess can turn learning into a chore.
Cohesion: Those who defend… Critics however… In my view.

prompt: Older people have little to teach younger generations in a fast-changing world. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think older people have little to teach younger generations in a fast-changing world. I do not agree with this idea.

It is true that the world changes fast, especially technology. In this area, young people often know more than older people, for example about phones and the internet.

However, older people still have a lot to teach. They have many years of experience in life and work. They can teach important things like values, patience, how to solve problems and how to deal with difficulties. These things never change.

In my opinion, older people still have valuable knowledge to share. In conclusion, I disagree that they have little to teach younger generations.
band8:
It is sometimes argued that, in a rapidly changing world, older people have little of value to teach the young. I strongly disagree: although technology has shifted some expertise to the young, the wisdom of older generations remains deeply valuable.

There is a grain of truth in the claim. In fields that change quickly, particularly digital technology, young people are often more fluent than their elders, and in this narrow sense the traditional flow of knowledge has reversed.

Yet to conclude that older people have little to offer is profoundly mistaken. Decades of lived experience equip them with insights that no amount of technological change renders obsolete: how to navigate relationships, cope with adversity, exercise patience and make sound judgements under pressure. These timeless lessons in character and wisdom cannot be downloaded; they are earned through years of living.

In my view, the ideal is mutual learning, with the young sharing technological fluency and the old sharing hard-won wisdom. In conclusion, I firmly disagree that older people have little to teach, since experience offers guidance that a fast-changing world makes more, not less, necessary.
gap: Strong disagreement that still concedes the tech point.
Lexis: lived experience, timeless lessons, mutual learning, hard-won wisdom.
Distinguishes fast-changing skills from enduring wisdom.
Proposes mutual/intergenerational learning.
Cohesion: There is a grain of truth… Yet… In my view.

prompt: Plastic waste is polluting the world's oceans. Why is this happening, and what can be done to reduce it?
band6:
Plastic waste is polluting the oceans. There are reasons for this and we can do some things to reduce it.

There are some reasons. People use a lot of single-use plastic like bottles and bags, and then they throw them away. In many places, waste is not collected or managed well, so the plastic goes into rivers and then the sea.

We can do some things to reduce it. People should use less plastic and use reusable bags and bottles. Governments can ban single-use plastic and improve recycling. Companies should also use materials that are better for the environment.

In conclusion, ocean plastic comes from single-use plastic and poor waste management, but using less plastic and better recycling can reduce it.
band8:
Plastic waste is choking the world's oceans, a crisis with clear causes that can nonetheless be tackled through determined action.

The causes are twofold. First, society is addicted to single-use plastics, bottles, bags, packaging and straws that are used for moments yet persist for centuries. Second, waste management in many regions is woefully inadequate, so that discarded plastic escapes into rivers and is carried out to sea, where it harms marine life and enters the food chain.

A range of measures could stem the tide. Individuals can cut their plastic use by choosing reusable bottles and bags and refusing unnecessary packaging. Governments can ban or tax single-use plastics, invest in proper recycling and waste collection, and hold manufacturers responsible for the packaging they produce. Crucially, industry must develop and adopt biodegradable alternatives at scale.

In conclusion, ocean plastic pollution arises chiefly from our reliance on single-use plastics and from poor waste management. Through reduced consumption, stronger regulation, better recycling and greener materials, however, this damage can be significantly reduced, protecting the oceans for the future.
gap: Both parts answered, with the two causes named precisely.
Lexis: single-use plastics, waste management, biodegradable, the food chain.
Solutions span individuals, government and industry.
'used for moments yet persist for centuries' sharpens the point.
Cohesion: The causes are twofold… A range of measures… In conclusion.

prompt: People should retire from work at the age of 60 to make room for younger workers. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
band6:
Some people think people should retire at 60 to make room for younger workers. I do not fully agree with this idea.

It is true that this idea has some benefits. When older people retire, young people can get their jobs. This reduces youth unemployment, which is a big problem in many countries.

However, there are problems. Many people at 60 are still healthy and able to work well. Forcing them to retire wastes their experience and skills. Also, some people need to work longer to earn enough money for their old age.

In my opinion, retirement should be a choice, not a fixed rule. In conclusion, I do not fully agree that everyone should retire at 60.
band8:
It is sometimes argued that people should retire at sixty to free up jobs for the young. While I understand the motive, I disagree that a compulsory retirement age of sixty is wise or fair.

The argument has some appeal. In countries plagued by youth unemployment, retiring older workers does, in theory, open positions for younger ones, giving them a foothold in the labour market and refreshing the workforce with new ideas.

However, the policy is deeply flawed. Many people at sixty remain healthy, capable and eager to work, and forcing them out squanders decades of valuable experience and expertise that benefit employers and colleagues alike. Compulsory early retirement can also leave people financially insecure, with too few years of saving to fund a long old age. Moreover, the assumption that jobs are fixed in number is questionable, since experienced workers often help create new roles rather than merely occupy old ones.

In my view, retirement should be a flexible choice, perhaps phased, rather than a rigid rule. In conclusion, I disagree that everyone should retire at sixty, since age alone is a poor measure of a person's value and needs.
gap: Disagrees with precise reasons (waste of experience, false fixed-jobs assumption).

prompt: Some think a tax on sugary drinks will improve public health, while others doubt it. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
band6:
Some people think a tax on sugary drinks will improve public health, while others doubt it. I will discuss both views.

Some people support the sugar tax. They say if sugary drinks are more expensive, people will buy fewer of them. This can reduce obesity and diseases like diabetes, and the tax money can be used for healthcare.

Other people doubt it. They say people who like sugary drinks will buy them anyway, even if they are more expensive. They also say the tax hurts poor people more, because they pay a bigger part of their income.

In my opinion, the sugar tax can help a little, but it must be used with other measures. In conclusion, both views are valid, and I think the tax is useful as one part of a bigger plan.
band8:
Whether a tax on sugary drinks improves public health is much debated, with supporters confident and sceptics unconvinced. Having weighed both views, I believe such a tax can help, but only as part of a wider strategy.

Supporters argue that raising the price of sugary drinks discourages consumption, much as tobacco taxes have reduced smoking. Lower intake of sugar could curb obesity and type-2 diabetes, while the revenue raised can fund health initiatives, a double benefit.

Sceptics, however, raise reasonable doubts. Habitual consumers may simply pay the higher price or switch to other unhealthy products, limiting the effect, and because the poor spend a larger share of their income on such goods, the tax is regressive, hitting them hardest. A single tax, they argue, cannot solve a complex problem rooted in diet and lifestyle.

In my view, the truth lies between these positions. A sugar tax is a useful tool, but not a cure on its own; combined with education, clearer labelling and curbs on advertising, it can contribute to better public health. In conclusion, I support the tax as one sensible element of a broader effort, rather than a solution in itself.
gap: Reaches a measured verdict (useful within a wider strategy).

Draws the tobacco-tax parallel to strengthen the supporting case.
Concedes the regressive and substitution objections fairly.
